coredata.dc:title: Dual-emitting fluorescence ratiometric nanoprobes of in-vitro/in-vivo pH from constructions to sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology C: Photochemistry Reviews
coredata.dc:creator: Gui, Rijun
Jin, Hui
coredata.dc:description: During the past two decades, there is an explosive growth of numerous scientific researches on dual-emitting fluorescence ratiometric nanoprobes (FRNPs). FRNPs adopt dual-signal ratiometry as signal output toward specific targets, and exhibit unique in-built signal self-calibration function and high accuracy for target detection. Especially, FRNPs can perform high-performance detection of in-vitro/in-vivo pH. These explored FRNPs-pH systems were extensively applied to pH-sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications at the levels of living cells and small animals. Thereby, this review has systematically summarized the promising FRNPs-pH systems reported in recent years. The first section has clearly introduced dual-emission fluorescence (FL) ratiometric modes and FL sensing mechanisms during the construction and application processes. The second section wholly summarizes various construction methods of FRNPs-pH systems based on different compositions and structures. The third section rationally discusses potential applications of FRNPs-pH systems, referring to FL bioimaging at the levels of living cells and small animals, pH-sensing and imaging detection at the levels of whole cells, organelles, tissues and organs, as well as FL imaging-guided tumor therapy. Furthermore, the current status, potential challenges and development perspectives in following researches are analyzed reasonably. This comprehensive and timely review promotes further explorations of multifunctional luminescent nanoprobes and versatile sensing materials for highly efficient biomedical applications.

(C) Schematic illustration of the nanoprobe for ratiometric FL detection of pH and cellular imaging based on N,S-CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the design of dual-emission CPDs for pH Sensing. (B) Schematic representation of POMF–MEHPPV Pdots for pH sensing. Synthesis of POMF: reaction conditions, 3-mercaptopropionic acid, 2,2′-azobis(2-methyl propionitrile) (AIBN), ethanol, 60 °C, 24 h. Preparation of POMF-MEHPPV Pdots. (C) A scheme showing the preparation for Pdots-PPF.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [34] with permission of 2021 American Chemical Society. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [36] with permission of 2021 IOP Publishing Ltd. (c) Reproduced from Ref. [35] with permission of 2019 by the authors, Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis scheme for MnQdots and the mechanism of ratiometric pH sensing by using DPA-MnQdots. (B) Schematic representation of the band diagram of CdSe/CdS DiB-NCs and pH sensing mechanism. At pH< 7, excess H+ cations quench the shell luminescence via directly removing electrons from the NCs conduction band and via depleting/activating electron accepting surface defects (SD). At pH> 7, excess OH− species passivate surface electron traps to promote radiative recombination of excitons. Demonstration of the ratiometric pH sensing ability of DiB-NCs. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the thioglycolic acid-capped CdSe/CdS DiBNCs in water at increasing pH (2−11) under 405 nm excitation (fluence 1 µJ cm−2). Spectrum corresponding to neutral condition (pH 7) is shown as a black line for reference. Inset shows a magnified view of the core emission band as a function of pH for clarity. Integrated intensities of core (red circles, I C) and shell (green circles, I S) emissions as a function of pH in two consecutive cycles (full and empty circles) to demonstrate a reversibility of pH sensing response. Ratio between the shell and core emission intensities (I S/I C) is reported as blue circles. Inset highlights acidic pH range (one cycle only for clarity), showing a two-fold variation of ratiometric response.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [42] with permission of 2016 Elsevier. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [44] with permission of 2017 WILEY-VCH

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of synthesizing dual-emission LysNP-AuNCs for ratiometric pH sensing via a combination of two synthetic strategies, lysozyme-mediated formation of AuNCs and glutaraldehyde-induced cross-linking of lysozyme-stabilized AuNCs. (B) Schematic illustrations for designing HPTS-PAH-AuNCs. FL spectra of the HPTS-PAH-AuNCs (0.5 mg/mL) at different pH values (3.0–9.0).

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [59] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2019. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [62] with permission of 2021 American Chemical Society

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustrations of energy-transfer nanoprobes and chemical structures of MOFs and polymers. pH sensing by using polymers and nanoprobes (λ ex = 420 nm). FL spectra of PBDR and MOF1-PBDR in different pH buffer solutions. Left axis: correlation between the emission intensity at 645 nm and pH of PBDR. Right axis: linear correlation between emission intensity ratio (F 645 nm/F 530 nm) and pH of MOF1-PBDR solution. (B) Structures of HTTA–Eu3+/Tb3+ mixture and the luminescence response principle toward pH. ICT; photographs show luminescence colors of the mixture in 0.05 M PBS buffers of pH (5.0, 7.4) under excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp. Time-resolved emission spectra (λ ex = 321 nm) of the mixture (1.0 M, HTTA/Eu3+/Tb3+ = 3/2/1) in 0.05 M PBS buffers with different pH values ranged from 4.8 to 8.6. The plot of I 540 nm/I 610 nm ratio against pH.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [48] with permission of 2019 Springer. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [56] with permission of 2012 Elsevier

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of carbon-dot-based FRET sensor for pH sensing. Photographs were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp at pH 4.5 (left) and pH 7.4 (right). FL emission spectra of CD–FITC (0.15 mg/mL) in 10 mM phosphate-buffered solutions at various pH values. The ratio of FL intensity of acceptor to that of donor (I 531/I 413) versus pH. A sigmoidal fit of the plot indicated a pK a of 5.69. (B) Synthesis route of CQD-TCPP and pH-response mechanism. Emission spectra of CQDs and CQD-TCPP, pH 6.5. The emission spectra of CQD-TCPP at different pH values, and the excitation wavelength is 405 nm.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [51] with permission of 2013 IOP Publishing Ltd. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [53] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2018.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of the nanoprobe formation and structures of RhB, DBZTC and Tpy-Cy. FL response of the nanoprobe with various pHs under 650 nm and 560 nm excitation. FL intensity ratio of F 750/F 575 as the function of pH. (B) Ratiometric fluorescent probe prepared based on Janus SiO2 NPs by Pickering emulsion method. Change of FL spectra with decreasing pH from 8.0 to 3.0 (λ ex = 488 nm). Ratiometric calibration curve showing peak sensor-emission intensity (at 530 nm) divided by peak reference-emission intensity (at 580 nm) vs pH value.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [75] with permission of 2017 American Chemical Society. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [77] with permission of 2019 Elsevier

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) A proposed schematic representation corresponding to the emission mechanism of Cdotsorange, followed by B-Cdots induced changes in emission mechanism of the same after composite formation (depicting blue-shift in Cdotsorange λ max). pH responses of the synthesized Cdot/PVA composite. PL spectra of the composite were recorded in 0.1 M PBS solution with varying pHs. Ratiometric changes in FL intensity of composite (I b/I or) under varying pHs (λ ex = 380 nm). (B) Schematic illustration for pH sensing by hybrid nanosensors. FL spectra of sensor 1-CDs-Lyso in DMSO-HEPES buffer solutions (1:100, v/v) of different pHapp values determined upon excitation at 380 nm. Linear relationship for ratiometric responses from the emission spectra of 1-CDs-Lyso to different pHapp values.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [27] with permission of 2019 WILEY-VCH. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [50] with permission of 2017 Chinese Chemical Society and Institute of Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Principle of the designed FITC-FPen/FPen@AuNC nanosensor for ratiometric extracellular pH sensing. Analysis performance of FITC-FPen@AuNC. FL spectra of the ratiometric nanosensor in buffer solution at varied pH values. Plot of I 521/I 635 vs. pH value. (B) The synthesis and surface functionalization of MSNs. Ratios of normalized FL intensities between the indicator (FITC) and reference (RITC) dyes in sodium phosphate buffers at varying pH 5.0–8.0. FL emissions from MSN-PP and MSN-TA were measured at peaks of 515 nm (FITC) and 575 nm (RITC), respectively.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [60] with permission of 2018 American Chemical Society. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [72] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2012.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 10

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic showing NA@Zr-BTB/F/R with NA capping Zr6 SBUs. FITC and RITC covalently linked to BTB-NH2 ligands. Excitation spectra (dotted lines) and emission spectra (solid lines) of NA@Zr-BTB/F/R after incubation with 10 mM of GSH at 37 °C for 15 min at NA (blue), FITC (green), and RITC (red) channels. Cationic NA@Zr-BTB/F/R ratiometrically senses dysregulated mitochondria with GSH depletion and matrix acidification. FITC emission spectra of NA@Zr-BTB/F/R at different pHs with 0 mM GSH. NA, FITC and RITC FL intensities of NA@ Zr-BTB/F/R acquired by a fluorimeter at different pHs (n = 3). Ratios of NA FL over RITC FL, and FITC FL over RITC FL for NA@Zr-BTB/F/R. These ratios serve as calibration curves for GSH/pH sensing. (B) Schematic of dual-fluorescent hSNF pH sensor and the optical characterization setup. Left upper inset is a microscope image of the tilted optical fiber tip positioned above the hSNFs. Emission spectrum of hSNFs coated with both FITC and Ru(BPY)3. Characterization of pH responses of the hSNFs. Optical spectra of hSNFs collected at different locations with error bars (the shaded area). Inserted figure indicates I FITC/I Ru(BPY)3 ratio at each location. Linear fitting of the I FITC/I Ru(BPY)3 ratio against different pHs.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [172] with permission of 2021 American Chemical Society (b) Reproduced from Ref. [178] with permission of 2023 The Authors. Published by The Royal Society of Chemistry

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 11

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FL images of HeLa cells treated by CDs (1.0 mg/mL) in bright-filed (top left), blue (top right), green (bottom left), and red (bottom right) channels. (B) Confocal fluorescent images (pseudocolor) during the process of cells proliferation and metabolism. Relationship of the ratio of I green/I red versus the time of cells proliferation and metabolism. Error bars represent standard error measurements.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [30] with permission of Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria 2020. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [83] with permission of 2015 Elsevier

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 12

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Visualization for pH changes in E. coli by confocal laser scanning microscopy at pH 2.5 and pH 7.4. Excitation wavelength is 405 nm. Emission is collected in blue and red channels. Scale bar is 10 µm. (B) LSCM images for zebrafish treated with CDs at pH 5 and pH 8. (C) FL images of tumor sites overlaid on the corresponding bright field images of tumor-bearing mice showing signal variations of both Cy5.5 and ANNA. Right frames (1 −9): heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, intestine, bone, muscle, and tumor.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [31] with permission of 2017 American Chemical Society. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [33] with permission of 2022 Elsevier. (c) Reproduced from Ref. [68] with permission of 2017 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 13

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Confocal images of HeLa cells treated with TBS solution at different pH values. Blue, red and merged images. Scale bars: 10 µm. Blue-to-red ratios of HeLa cells at various pH values. Seven cells were selected and the average FL intensities for two separated channels used to calculate the data using Image J. (B) Bright field (A1-D1) and ratiometric FL (A2- D2) images of HeLa cells incubated with CDs-pH (20 mg/mL) for 30 min at 37 ℃ with different pH values (5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0). Ratiometric images of HeLa cells were analyzed by Image J. FL images were collected in 500–570 nm and 600–670 nm with λ ex = 488 nm. Color band on the bottom represents the variation of FL ratio change with pH. Scale bar: 20 µm. Intracellular pH calibration curve of CDs-pH. (C) Ratiometric calibration of pH in living cells. Confocal FL images of 0.5 mg/mL CDs incubating with HeLa cells in high-K+ HEPES-buffered solution in the presence of nigericin at different pH. Calibration curve of intracellular pH sensing.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [45] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [24] with permission of 2019 Chinese Chemical Society and Institute of Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences. (c) Reproduced from Ref. [25] with permission of 2016 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Intracellular distribution of Eu@SiNRs. Confocal images of the Eu@SiNRs in live HeLa and MCF-7 cells. Cell lines are loaded with 500 μg/mL of Eu@SiNRs at 37 °C for 12 h. Scale bars: 25 µm. Inset images (I−IV) represent the enlarged single HeLa and MCF-7 cell. Scale bars: 10 µm. (B) Distribution profiles for FL intensity of Eu@SiNRs along the diameter of a single HeLa and MCF-7 cell. Insert I presents single HeLa cell, and Insert II presents the corresponding histograms of mean FL intensity in nucleus and cytoplasm. Intracellular pH calibration via Eu@SiNRs. CLSM images of Eu@SiNRs in HeLa cells (C) and MCF-7 cells (E) at pH values of 3.0 (I), 4.0 (II), 5.0 (III), 6.0 (IV), 7.0 (V), 8.0 (VI), or 9.0 (VII). The Eu@SiNRs (500 μg/mL)-treated cells were respectively incubated with highly concentrated K+ buffer containing 10 μM nigericin with different pH values at 37 °C for 30 min. The bottom color strip represents the pseudocolor changing with pH. Scale bars: 25 µm. The corresponding histograms of FL intensity ratio ( R = I 470/I 620) versus cytoplasmic pH values ranging from 3 to 9 in HeLa cells (D) and MCF-7 cells (F). Inset presents the linear relationship between R and pH values in the range of 4–9. Error bars show the standard deviations determined from three independent measurements.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Ref. [46] with permission of 2017 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 15

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: pH-responsive FL imaging. (Top left) FL imaging of PLGA-ICG-SiRB dispersed in serum solutions with different pHs. (A) FL intensities of ICG and SiRB under different pHs. (B) I 650/I 830 intensity ratios of PLGA-ICG- SiRB measured under different pHs. (Center left) Ex-vivo FL images of major organs and tumor dissected from the mice i.v. injected with PLGA-ICG-SiRB (24 h post-injection). Li: liver, Sp: spleen, Ki: kidney, H: heart, Lu: lung, T: tumor. (C) FL intensity at 650 nm and 830 nm. (D) I 650/I 830 intensity ratio in the tumor and other major organs measured. (Bottom left) Ex-vivo FL images of the tumor with different sizes collected from the mice i.v. injected with PLGA-ICG-SiRB (24 h post-injection). (E) FL intensity at 650 nm and 830 nm. (F) I 650/I 830 intensity ratio in the tumors measured.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Ref. [81] with permission of 2018 Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 16

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration for the chemical structure and the proposed mechanism of RPP (i.e. RhB-PEG8- K(PpIX)) toward ratiometric pH sensing and tumor-targeted photodynamic therapy. (B) PDT therapeutic effects of RPP. With or without light irradiation, cytotoxicity of RPP against: (left) 4T1 cells at pH 7.4, (middle) 4T1 cells at pH 6.0, and (right) COS7 cells at pH 7.4. Flow cytometry analysis of 4T1 cells treated using RPP with or without light irradiation at pH 7.4 or pH 6.0. CLSM images of 4T1 cells treated by RPP with or without light irradiation at pH 7.4 or pH 6.0 and stained by Calcein-AM/PI. Scale bar: 100 µm. (C) Anti-tumor study of RPP. Relative tumor volume changes after treatments with PBS, RPP, or “RPP plus laser irradiation”. The corresponding average tumor weight, the sacrificed tumor images and H&E staining analysis of tumor tissues after various treatments at the 13th day. Scale bar: 100 µm. Relative body weight of the mice after various treatments in 13 days. *P < 0.05 and ** P < 0.01 were tested through a Student’s t-test.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Ref. [182] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2019.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 17

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Cell viability for HeLa cells in the presence of different concentrations of CDs. (B) Cell viability of HeLa cells (left) and MCF-7 cells (right) treated with Eu@SiNRs with different concentrations for various incubation times by the use of MTT assay. Morphologies of HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells treated with Eu@SiNRs of different concentrations (31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500 μg/mL) for 12 h (I−VI) and 24 h (VII−XII). (C) Viability from T24 cells and 7702 cells incubated with BQD-FAs at different concentrations after exposure to laser (660 nm, 50 mW) irradiation for 6 min. (D) Viability of HeLa cells with different concentrations of AuNC@F-mSiO2 @ R-copolymer carrier, CPT and carrier-CPT with or without laser irradiation at 808 nm. MTT assays of D-CDs for (a) MCF-7 and (b) HEK-293 cell lines, (c, d) FESEM images of the untreated and treated MCF-7 cells.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [25] with permission of 2016 American Chemical Society (b) Reproduced from Ref. [46] with permission of 2017 American Chemical Society. (c) Reproduced from Ref. [43] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2020. (d) Reproduced from Ref. [79] with permission of 2014 WILEY-VCH. (E) (e) Reproduced from Ref. [22] with permission of 2022 American Chemical Society

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 18

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) pH titration curve of probe-labeled SMMC-7721 cells. The pseudocolored ratiometric image of probe- labeled SMMC-7721 cells (top center) and single SMMC-7721 cells (top right). (middle left) Probe-labeled A-549 cells. (middle) Pseudocolored ratiometric image of probe-labeled A-549 cells. Estimated pH values at the region of interest indicated by black circle in (middle left). (bottom left) The probe-labeled BEAS-2B cells. (bottom middle) Pseudocolored ratiometric image of probe-labeled BEAS-2B cells. Estimated pH values at the region of interest indicated by black circle in (bottom left). (B) Tumor- targeting behaviors of RPP via EPR effects. Real-time FL images for 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse after intravenous injection by RPP. Circle of dots represent the tumor tissue. Ex-vivo FL images and the corresponding relative mean FL intensity (MFI) analysis of sacrificed heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor tissue at 32 h after injection with RPP. Excitation wavelength is 630 nm.

ce:figure.ce:source: (a) Reproduced from Ref. [77] with permission of 2019 Elsevier. (b) Reproduced from Ref. [182] with permission of The Royal Society of Chemistry 2019

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of this review about dual-emitting FRNPs-pH from experimental constructions to sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications in vitro and in vivo.
Schematic illustration for FRNPs-pH systems referring to various dual-emission FL ratiometric modes and construction methods.
Brief summaries and comparisons from various types of nanomaterials to construct FRNPs-pH systems.

head.ce:title: Dual-emitting fluorescence ratiometric nanoprobes of in-vitro/in-vivo pH from constructions to sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Intellectual Property Research Institute, Qingdao University, Shandong 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Intellectual Property Research Institute, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Intellectual Property Research Institute, Qingdao University, Shandong 266071, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: R. Gui and H. Jin have the equivalent contribution to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the past two decades, there is an explosive growth of numerous scientific researches on dual-emitting fluorescence ratiometric nanoprobes (FRNPs). FRNPs adopt dual-signal ratiometry as signal output toward specific targets, and exhibit unique in-built signal self-calibration function and high accuracy for target detection. Especially, FRNPs can perform high-performance detection of in-vitro/in-vivo pH. These explored FRNPs-pH systems were extensively applied to pH-sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications at the levels of living cells and small animals. Thereby, this review has systematically summarized the promising FRNPs-pH systems reported in recent years. The first section has clearly introduced dual-emission fluorescence (FL) ratiometric modes and FL sensing mechanisms during the construction and application processes. The second section wholly summarizes various construction methods of FRNPs-pH systems based on different compositions and structures. The third section rationally discusses potential applications of FRNPs-pH systems, referring to FL bioimaging at the levels of living cells and small animals, pH-sensing and imaging detection at the levels of whole cells, organelles, tissues and organs, as well as FL imaging-guided tumor therapy. Furthermore, the current status, potential challenges and development perspectives in following researches are analyzed reasonably. This comprehensive and timely review promotes further explorations of multifunctional luminescent nanoprobes and versatile sensing materials for highly efficient biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of important physicochemical parameters, pH is termed as the index of H+ concentration and the sign of H+ activity in solution. pH value is well known as the acid-base value, and the pH value of healthy human body is 7.35–7.45. pH plays a crucial role in environmental, chemical and biological processes. Particularly at the level of living cells, the intracellular pH plays a vital role in multiple physiological processes, including cell metabolism, enzyme activity, homeostasis, ion transport, etc. Commonly, different components from cells have respective pH value ranges, such as lysosomes and endosomes (pH 4.5–6.0), Golgi apparatus (pH 6.4–6.8), cytoplasm (pH 6.8–7.6), nucleus (pH ∼7.6), mitochondria (pH 7.8–7.9) [1–5]. Abnormal acidic pH is one of key characters about cellular dysfunctions, involving cell apoptosis, cancer, Alzheimer’s diseases, etc. A reduced pH (4.0–6.0) frequently occurs in endosomes and lysosomes originating from different metabolic pathways. Intracellular acidification in cytoplasm is related to the factor of therapies or multi-drug resistance. pH can regulate the capability of ion transport, cell proliferation or migration. Cellular phagocytosis, endocytosis or receptor-mediated internalization is often accompanied by alteration of pH value, and so cellular internalization process can be studied by regulating or monitoring pH. Acidic pH (6.2–6.9) in cells serves as the indicator of latent malignant tumors because of enhanced glycolysis and poor perfusion in most solid tumors. Acidic pH facilitates tumor growth, invasion and metastasis. Inflammation and chemotherapy are much associated with acidic intracellular pH. As a key acid-base balance parameter, cell survival depends on different biochemical interactions of biologically active macromolecules under controllable pH, and pH variations in homeostasis of intracellular environment usually occur at the early stage of diseases [6–10].

Disrupted homeostasis of pH in the entire cells or particular organelles probably induces serious damage, including generation of free radicals, disruption of membrane contractility, inappropriate cell apoptosis, necrosis, etc. pH variations in cells and organelles are also related to the occurrence and development of serious diseases and even malignant tumors. Hence, both precise regulation of intracellular pH and effective monitoring of pH fluctuation at organelle levels are much critical for accurate understanding of diverse cellular functions and pathological processes. To realize highly sensitive and selective determination of pH, rational exploration of efficient pH-analysis methods and technologies is of great significance in scientific researches and practical applications. When compared to traditional pH electrodes for electrochemical detection (pH-meter principle), optical pH sensors based on fluorescent indicators such as inorganic materials and organic dyes as probes, show many unique advantages [11–15]. Fluorescent probes of pH enable direct use without direct contact of sensing element with read-out device, and are normally operated within strong magnetic fields and high pressures. Nanoscale sensors or molecular probes of pH can be distributed in living cells, tissues and organs of small animals toward in-vitro/in-vivo sensing and imaging monitoring of pH in a noninvasive way. Besides, fluorescence (FL) probes can be miniaturized to a nanoscale level. FL nanoprobes of pH exhibit a capability of highly sensitive detection of pH at microscopic levels in living cells and small animals.

In previous studies, numerous reports have involved various analysis methods for pH detection based on fluorescent probes [1–15], and most of them adopted a single-signal detection mode that depends on the measurement of single-signal intensity (SSI) to construct the relationship between SSI and pH. However, the measurement process of SSI is often disturbed by pH-irrelevant factors, such as the influence from background signals, fluctuation of probe contents, alteration of sample conditions, etc. To conquer the limitations, a signal self-calibration method is adopted to explore a dual-signal ratiometric detection mode [16–20]. Dual-signal ratiometric probes with built-in signal correction character usually use a ratio of dual-signal intensities at respective signal-peak positions to establish a relationship between the ratio (not SSI) and pH. This type of probes can maximally avoid potential effects from pH-irrelevant factors, and thus ensure high accuracy and practicability during pH-detection process. The past decade has witted explosive development in FL ratiometric probes of pH based on various types of fluorescent inorganic materials, organic dyes or inorganic- organic hybrid systems as briefly summarized in both Table 1 [21–49] and Table 2 [50–84]. These functionalized probes possess significant and excellent bio-applications, especially in-vitro/in-vivo sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications. To better facilitate the ever-increasing studies about FL ratiometric probes of pH toward promising applications, a comprehensive review shows great significance for scientific researchers, but it is challenging and still lacking currently. This unique context reveals a rational motivation for relevant researchers to timely and efficiently accomplish this review work that is of scientific urgency and necessary.

Before this present review, we wholly compared previously published reviews about fluorescent pH sensors and probes. Typically, Shamsipur et al. introduced design methods and applications of fluorescent nanosensors of pH [2]. Hande et al. reported recent advances in small molecule-based intracellular pH probes [3]. Méndez-Ardoy et al. involved synthesis and supramolecular functional assemblies for ratiometric pH probes [4]. Wen et al. highlighted recent progress of organic small molecule-based fluorescent probes for intracellular pH sensing [7]. Costanzo et al. analyzed visual pH sensors from chemical perspective to new bio-engineered materials [8]. Chen reviewed recent advances in fluorescent probes for extracellular pH detection and imaging [10]. Yue et al. offered a review to describe the progress in pH probes for cell applications [14]. Previous reviews mention fluorescent pH sensors and probes, but a review about fluorescent ratiometric sensors and probes of pH is still lacking so far. In view of this specific context and scientific significance, we conduct a review about recent progress on FL ratiometric nanoprobes of pH (FRNPs-pH). In this review, we have systematically summarized FRNPs-pH, focusing on construction methods of nanoprobe systems, ratiometric modes, sensing mechanisms, in-vitro/vivo sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications ( Scheme 1). Representative studies referring to FRNPs-pH published during the past two decades are highlighted. Current status, probable challenges and perspectives in this scientific direction are also discussed rationally.

On the basis of literature analysis, ratiometric modes of FRNPs-pH include two common types. (1) In a system of FRNPs-pH, one FL-signal (F1) is pH-insensitive and serves as a reference, while another FL-signal (F2) is pH-sensitive and specifically responds to pH. Two signals are transferred into a ratiometric output (F1/F2 or F2/F1). This ratiometric output (or its functional forms, such as logarithmic form) is used to construct a functional (linear frequently) relationship of F1/F2 (F2/F1) with pH. (2) In a system of FRNPs-pH, double FL signals (F1, F2) can sensitively and specifically respond to pH, and are transferred into a ratiometric readout mode (F1/F2 or F2/F1) to construct a functional relationship of F1/F2 (F2/F1) versus pH. According to a plotted functional relationship of dual-FL ratiometric value and pH, the resulting FRNPs-pH can perform a built-in self-calibration pH detection. Especially, FRNPs-pH enables high-performance detection and quantification of pH in complex environmental and biological samples, showing superior signal-to-background (noise) and reliable accuracy. As shown in Table 1 and Table 2, previous studies about FRNPs-pH refer to various FL ratiometric modes, extensively referring to “F1↑/F2↓”, “F1↓/F2 ↑”, “F1↑/F2↑”, “F1↓/F2↓”, “F1↑/F2↔”, “F1↓/F2↔”, “F1↔/F2↑” and “F1↔/F2↓”, where “↑”, “↓” and “↔” respectively indicate the increasing, decreasing and almost unchanged FL-peak intensities. The last-four FL ratiometric modes correspond to one common type of FRNPs-pH (1), and the first-four modes correspond to another common type of FRNPs-pH (2). More details are available in Scheme 2.

As for FRNPs-pH (1), compared to pH-sensitive FL as response signals, the reference FL from intrinsic fluorophors in FRNPs-pH or externally added fluorophors outside of FRNPs-pH exhibits negligible changes caused by pH. In comparison with single-FL detection mode, the one-reference FL ratiometric mode possesses the enhanced accuracy and reproducibility for pH detection. As for FRNPs-pH (2), pH variation generally triggers the increase of one FL-signal, accompanied by the decrease of another FL-signal. Except for dual-FL changes in opposite direction, pH variation can induce dual-FL changes in the same direction, regardless of different varying degrees for dual-FL signals. Moreover, two FL signals are often derived from FRNPs-pH before pH variation. Besides, pH variation can also cause the reduction (or even disappearance) of one FL-signal, together with the emergence and enhancement of another FL-signal, because of pH variation-triggered chemical reactions, physical interactions, energy/charge transfer, etc. Among various FL ratiometric modes of FRNPs-pH, when dual-FL signals are transferred into a ratio, the ratio should markedly respond to pH. A plotted equation of the ratio versus pH is required to possess a significant positive slope, such as “F1↑/F2↓”, “F1↑/F2↔” and “F1↔/F2↓”. By contrast, “F1↑/F2↓” with dual-FL signal changes in opposite direction can distinctly improve the sensitivity of the ratio responding to pH, implying an improved signal-to-background and superior anti-interference capability.

In addition to the above common types, previous studies refer to other pH ratiometric modes of FRNPs-pH. Upon excitation at a specific wavelength, FRNPs-pH systems can emit two FL signals before or after pH variations. Particularly, FRNPs-pH systems are excited at a certain wavelength to emit one FL signal (E1), and are excited at a different wavelength to emit another FL signal (E2). E1 or/and E2 are pH-sensitive, and are transferred into a ratio (E1/E2 or E2/E1) to construct a curve relationship between the specific ratio versus pH. Therein, the dual-excitation method is employed to produce two different FL outputs, which are transferred into a particular FL ratiometric mode to perform pH sensing [62,85–97]. The specific ratio (like E1/E2) is usually converted into a modified form of (E1/E2)0/(E1/E2) or (E1/E2)/(E1/E2)0, where (E1/E2)0 and (E1/E2) are respectively assigned to the specific ratio values before and after pH variations [91–93]. Notably, as for the above common types of FRNPs-pH (1, 2), the so-called FL ratiometric modes established by only one FL change without another FL as a reference are not in the scope of this review, such as (F1)0/(F1), (F1)/(F1)0, or [(F1)–(F1)0]/(F1)0, where (F1)0 and (F1) indicate F1-peak intensities before and after pH variations, respectively [98]. Considering that (F1)0 and (F1) are not measured at the same time, different from common FL ratiometric modes of FRNPs-pH (1, 2), the ratio modes between (F1)0 and (F1) cannot eliminate the influence from pH-irrelevant factors during each detection process.

During pH-sensing and imaging process, some of FRNPs-pH systems do not clearly provide the respective FL peak wavelengths, but indicate the corresponding FL colors or FL emission regions to establish dual-FL ratiometric modes, such as F green/F blue [23,57,84], F blue/F orange [27], F blue/F green [50], F red/F blue [57,84], F green/F red [58,74,78], F blue/F red [78,83], F green/F blue [84], F 618–628 nm/F 678–689 nm [47], etc. Sometimes, previous studies only provide two fluorophor names and chemical structures of FRNPs-pH to describe FL ratiometric modes, rather than their respective emission wavelengths, such as F FITC/F RBITC [52], F ANNA/F Cy5.5 [68], F FITC/F RITC [72], F Cy3/F Cy5 [82], and F core/F shell [44]. In addition, FRNPs-pH systems can simultaneously produce fluorescence (F), phosphorescence (P) or upconversion luminescence under different excitation conditions [66,99,100], which are used to design a particular dual-signal ratio among them, such as F 520 nm/P 710 nm [66]. Due to obvious UV–visible absorption spectrum characteristics of pH-sensitive organic fluorophors or dyes in FRNPs- pH systems, the dual-FL ratiometric sensing process of pH is often accompanied by the changes of two absorption peak intensities, which are used to explore a dual-absorbance (A) ratiometric mode to perform dual-A ratiometric sensing of pH [32,56,81]. As for a particular system of FRNPs-pH, it can even perform FL ratiometric sensing for both pH and other targets, such as Hg2+ [30], Fe3+ [32], Cu2+ [57,101], Cd2+ [102], L-lysine [31,33], urea [59], matrix metalloprotease-9 [68], O2 •− [75,78], oxygen pressure [84], temperature [48], etc. These unique characteristics endow FRNPs-pH with versatility for efficient biosensing, bioimaging and biomedical applications.

In this review, the FRNPs-pH systems are composed of fluorescent nanomaterials and inorganic -organic hybrids, rather than FL ratiometric probes from organic small molecules, supramolecules or functionalized assemblies [3,4,6,7]. The sensing mechanisms of FRNPs-pH usually involve pH- induced Förster or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), self-quenching effects of fluorophors, pH-activatable FL of fluorophors, pH-induced aggregation, energy level variation, transition between protonation and deprotonation, proton transfer, electron scavenging, etc. FRET indicates a nonradiative energy transfer process between energy donors in the excited state to energy acceptors in the ground state. When FL emission spectra of inorganic or organic fluorophors (as donors) are largely overlapped with absorption spectra of another component (as acceptors) in FRNPs-pH systems, the excitation of fluorophor donors causes the acceptors to emit photoluminescence or enhance it, together with FL reduction of the donors. Due to long-range dipole-dipole interactions, FRET causes the excited acceptors to emit in a longer-emission wavelength or relax in a nonradiative way. FRET efficiency mainly depends on the extent of spectral overlap, quantum yield of the donors, relative orientation of the donor-acceptor (D-A) transition dipoles, and D-A spatial distance. Generally, a larger-scale spectral overlap and a shorter D-A spatial distance in the range of 1–10 nm facilitate the realization of a higher FRET efficiency. The pH-sensing processes in FRNPs-pH systems extensively refer to FRET mechanisms in previous studies [29,36,37,39,48,51,68,76].

PET is an excited-state electron transfer process, accompanied with charge separation and redox reaction. PET usually happens through the transfer of electrons from the conduction band to the acceptor units, or through the donation of electrons from the donor units to the valence band holes. PET process may proceed upon the presence of electron acceptors or electron donor units attached (or closely approached) to fluorophors. The process can compete with radiative decay of exciton to the ground state, which therefore leads to an obvious reduction of FL quantum yield. Upon light excitation, the donor units do not emit or only emit weakly, which are ascribed to electron transfer from donor units to fluorophors at the excited state. When PET is blocked, the photoluminescence of fluorophors is restored. The previously explored FRNPs-pH systems often refer to PET during the process of pH-sensing applications [61,103–107].

ICT is widely utilized as a typical pH-sensing mechanism of FRNPs-pH systems [56,107–129]. In general, ICT transition involves a charge transferring from one certain part of molecules, or the electronic transition process of most charges. The part of giving charges is electron donor, and that of receiving charges is electron acceptor. When the electron acceptor comes from another part of the same molecule, the electronic transition process is termed as ICT process. Particular examples refer to electron D-A complexes, polychromatic molecules, etc. If charge transfer occurs between two molecules, it is called as intermolecular (rather than intramolecular) charge transfer. ICT acts as one of significant visual mechanisms to design biosensing dyes and FL imaging applications. If donor units of ICT dye molecules can specifically respond to the electron-absorbing species, such as carbonyl or acyl groups, ICT process will be restrained dramatically and FL quenching happens inevitably. The specific responses severely weaken the signal-to-noise ratio and sensitivity of ICT fluorophores, especially in accurate sensing and bio-labeling applications.

pH-triggered transition between protonation and deprotonation is usually used as one of typical mechanisms for FRNPs-pH systems in pH-sensing process [22,28,30,42,45,46,53,54]. pH-induced protonation-deprotonation transition is derived from various functional groups of organic ligands attached (capped/labeled/conjugated/assembled, etc.) on the surface of fluorescent nanomaterials, such as quinolyl nitrogen, pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen moieties, carboxylic acid groups, amino acid residues, etc. pH-sensitive and reversible transitions between protonation and deprotonation often occur in FL ratiometric pH probes based on organic fluorophors, such as electron D-A complexes or polychromatic molecules [108,113,115–117,120,123,126–128,130,131–142]. The transitions caused by pH variations are originating from pH-sensitive fluorophors in probe systems, such as phenolic hydroxyls in D-π-A-π-D conjugates, quinoline nitrogen in quinoline-hydrazones D-A molecules, N-H in heterocycle fused-naphthalimide ring systems, hydroxyl groups and benzoindole nitrogen moieties in hemicyanine skeletons, hydroxyl/carbonyl/indole groups, quinoline rings, etc.

In view of smaller Stoke’s shift and larger overlap between the absorption and emission spectra of fluorophors, the self-assembling and aggregation actions of fluorophors-based sensing systems can also cause FL quenching responses through the non-radiative energy transfer process [2]. This unique mechanism majorly mentions the structural transformation of pH-responsive polymerized particles, micelles or inorganic/organic hybridized structures into the functionalized conjugates or assemblies containing fluorophors. Specially, the pH-sensing mechanisms for FRNPs-pH based on fluorescent nanomaterials often refer to the changes of energy levels, aggregation, proton transfer and protective shell of surface ligands [12]. Frequently, pH-sensing process of FRNPs-pH systems is accompanied by the formation of surface states [23], phenol/phenolate, carboxylic/carboxylate, pyrrole/pyridine equilibrium [27,28,33], non-covalent interactions [30], self-polymerization [38], complex-induced aggregation, surface-state electron transfer [32], temperature-depended emission [34,63], electronic defects [44], electrostatic charging/attraction [46,62], etc. When the FRNPs-pH systems are composed of two or more fluorophors, such as fluorescent inorganic-organic hybrids, electron D-A complexes or polychromatic molecules, the pH-detection processes probably include multiple sensing mechanisms, as well as various adjustable electronic transfer, decomposition and reconstruction of chemical structures, intra/inter-molecular interactions, etc.

In this review, we have systematically summarized various construction methods of FRNPs-pH. The promising nanoprobe systems are generally composed of fluorescent nanomaterials, inorganic -organic fluorescent hybrids, and their abundant functionalized derivatives that show core/shelled, composited, mixed, assembled, or other complicated and progressive structures. As for a particular FRNPs-pH system, herein some of components can be considered as inorganic nanomaterials with one or more FL emission peaks, or belong to fluorophors-containing organic systems, such as the electron D-A complexes and polychromatic molecules. These resulting FRNPs-pH systems enable dual-FL emissions and FL-emission (peak-intensity/wavelength-shift) changes under pH variation, which thus facilitates the achievement of pH-responsive FL-signal output and dual-FL ratiometric detection mode toward signal self-calibration pH-sensing applications, showing superior detection stability and reproducibility. The following sections comprehensively analyze and discuss various construction methods of FRNPs-pH systems (Scheme 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of nanomaterials to construct FRNPs-pH
ce:section.ce:para: FRNPs-pH systems can be constructed through dual-FL emitting functional nanomaterials with pH-responsive FL, majorly including carbon dots or nanodots (CDs), polymer nanoparticles (NPs) or dots, graphene (oxide) or semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs), noble metal nanoclusters (NCs), rare-earth metal-ion complexes, etc. Compared with small organic molecules as fluorescent molecular probes, functional nanomaterials-based fluorescent nanoprobes show many advantages, such as the introduction of nanomaterials’ unique functions beyond FL (e.g., magnetic, acoustic or thermal properties), multiple modifications of nanomaterials with pH-responsive fluorophors and organic dye molecules (easily to design dual-FL ratiometric outputs in broad pH-sensitive ranges). Furthermore, some functional molecules (such as biocompatible polymers, bioactive molecules, etc.) can be employed as particular surface capping or stabilizing reagents to perform the surface functionalized modifications of nanomaterials, so as to improve the FL-emission efficiency of the resulting dual-FL emitting nanomaterials. Then, the functionalized nanomaterials are further used to construct the FRNPs-pH systems that exhibit superior performances in FL sensing and imaging applications. After surface functionalizations, fluorescent nanomaterials possess brighter FL and higher photo-stability, and are much suitable for applications in quantitative sensing and imaging analysis. However, functional nanomaterials-based fluorescent nanoprobes have some drawbacks, especially including latent microscopic heterogeneity and particle aggregation during the process of sensing and imaging applications. The drawbacks can be efficiently conquered by performing functionally multiple modifications on the surface of nanomaterials. In the following sections, we introduce various construction methods for fluorescent nanomaterials-based FRNPs-pH systems, and methodically discuss the each method through analyzing representative and state-of-the-art related studies (Table 1).

Fluorescent carbon NPs, especially including CDs, carbon nanodots, carbon QDs or carbonized graphene (oxide) QDs, exhibit dual-FL emission behaviors and pH-sensitive FL responses, which can be utilized to fabricate FL ratiometric detection modes for pH sensing [21–33,40,41,143,144]. Carbon NPs, such as CDs, are generally composed of sp 2/sp 3 hybrid carbons that contain abundant functional groups or organic polymers on the surface. Except for the major constituent element of carbon, heteroatoms (nitrogen, sulfur, boron, etc.) are often doped into CDs to achieve tunable and enhanced FL emission properties, including superior anti-blinking and photo-stability. CDs have small particle sizes (< 10 nm in general), broad source of raw materials (chemicals, biomass, etc.), low cost of preparation, ultralow photobleaching and toxicity, excellent compatibility, strong anti- interference from background signal and auto-FL. In terms of these advantages, pH-dependent FL properties of CDs indicate potential in-vitro/in-vivo sensing and imaging applications.

As summarized in Table 1, fluorescent carbon NPs can be primarily prepared through bottom- up and top-down methods. In a particular bottom-up method, one or more chemicals and biomass (citric acid, glutathione, formamide, ethylenediamine, basic fuchsin, hydrazine hydrate, 8-amino quinol, 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid, 1,2,4,5-benzene tetramine tetrahydrochloride, Agaricus bisporus etc.) can serve as carbon-resource raw materials to perform hydrothermal, solvothermal, chemical oxidation, pyrolysis and microwave-assisted process, to yield CDs with the doping of heteroatoms. In a typical top-down method, carbon NPs, such as (heteroatoms-doping) graphene and graphene oxide QDs, can be synthesized through adopting electrochemical approach, arc discharge, plasma treatment and laser ablation, using the bulk carbon materials as precursors. Li et al. prepared CDs through one-pot hydrothermal method by using 8-aminoquinol and citric acid as (N, C) precursors [22]. N-doped CDs show pH-dependent FL, because quinolyl nitrogen coating on the surface can be protonated or deprotonated upon the combination with H+ or OH– [145,146]. There is a gradual red-shifted FL peak with acidity rising, because of the proronation of quinolyl nitrogen ( Fig. 1 A). There is a sigmoidal fitting between the ratio (F 365 nm/F 439 nm) of dual-FL emission-peak intensities and pH. The CDs-based ratiometric FL probe was applied to cell-imaging labeling and monitoring of intracellular pH changes.

By one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and basic fuchsin as the precursors, Shangguan et al. prepared dual-FL emitting CDs, and explored CDs-based FRNPs-pH system for intracellular pH sensing [25]. The doped small molecule basic fuchsin shows FL-sensitive behavior. Citric acid acting as carbon source was used to prepare CDs with functional carboxyl/hydroxyl groups on the surface. The pH-triggered protonation-deprotonation transitions induce pH-sensitive FL responses. Thereby, the label-free CDs exhibit pH-responsive two emissions and realize FL ratiometric mode (F 475 nm/F 545 nm) for the detection of intracellular pH (Fig. 1B). Guo et al. prepared N,S dual-doped CDs via one-step hydrothermal steps of o-phenylenediamine and p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid as precursors [28]. pH-sensitivity is due to a protonation/deprotonation transition of pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen moieties [147,148]. At acidic pH, nitrogen on pyridine is protonated. Pyrrole nitrogen is also protonated with further enhanced acidity (Fig. 1 C), accompanied with FL decrease at 563 nm. Due to ICT effects between functional groups on the surface of N,S-CDs, a new FL emission peak appears at 645 nm. A corresponding linear regression equation of the FL ratio (F 563 nm/F 645 nm) vs. pH is obtained to develop FRNPs-pH toward pH visual sensing and cellular imaging.

Hai et al. prepared folic acid (FA)-encapsulated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via an one-pot microwaved alkali cutting method, using graphene oxide and FA as raw materials [41]. The GQDs show two FL emissions, which are attributed to different distributions of emission sites and sizes. The emission at 360 nm is derived from the carbonization of FA, while that at 450 nm is due to π-π* transition of aromatic sp 2 domains. In alkali condition, the GQDs are prone to form much free edge-enriched zigzag sites, causing strong FL. When zigzag sites are protonated and combine with H+ to form reversible complex, the emitting triple carbene states from intrinsic structures of GQDs are broken [149], together with inactive FL and alkali pH-induced FL decrease. FA-GQDs realize ratiometric (F 360 nm/F 450 nm) pH-sensing and multi-color imaging in living cells. N-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (N-GOQDs) were synthesized through solvothermal (N,N-dimethylformamide) treatment of GO micro-sheets as raw materials [40]. An emission band around 520 nm is assigned to the doped nitrogen, while that around 430 nm is ascribed to the size effects of sp 2 clusters [150]. The pH-dependant FL ratio (F 520 nm/F 430 nm) properties of N-GOQDs were explored as ratiometric intracellular pH sensors. Hence, fluorescent carbon NPs can be used to explore FRNPs-pH toward efficient dual-emitting FL ratiometric detection of pH [150,151]. Notably, single-emitting carbon NPs often combine with inorganic luminescent nanomaterials or organic fluorophores to construct dual-emitting ratiometric nanoprobe systems for built-in self-calibration sensing and visualization of components related to organisms, as well as biomedical applications [151–153].

According to previous studies about the construction of FRNPs-pH systems based on polymer dots (Pdots) and polymer NPs, pH-sensitive dyes and fluorophores are often mixed with functional polymers, followed by conducting (hydrothermal, solvothermal, reactions, etc.) treatments to yield carbonized Pdots that enable dual-emitting FL ratiometric responses on pH. Fluorescent polymers combine with pH-sensitive organic dyes, followed by the re-precipitation of NPs to produce dual- emission Pdots. After polymerization reactions of pH-sensitive functional monomers, fluorescent polymers are prepared. Then, polymers are further processed into fluorescent organic NPs through carbonization treatments, and the organic NPs can be explored as FRNPs-pH systems. Sometimes, instead of polymerization of pH-sensitive functional monomers, the pH-sensitive fluorescent dyes, fluorophores, functional groups and units are directly grafted, coupled or anchored on the skeleton of polymers to construct various types of functionalized Pdots or polymer NPs, which facilitate the exploration of FRNPs-pH systems with dual-FL emissions. pH-sensitive emissions come from fluorescent dyes and fluorophores in the matrix of Pdots or polymer NPs, or come from particular polymers with pH-sensitive functional groups and units. In general, FRNPs-pH systems prepared from polymers, Pdots and polymer NPs are composed of one pH-sensitive emission and another pH-insensitive emission, or even two pH-sensitive emissions [34–39,154,155,156].

Fluorescent polymer dots are widely applied to fabricate FRNPs-pH systems. Hu et al. utilized ethylene imine polymer (PEI) and 4,4′,4′’,4′’’-(porphine-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrakis(benzoic acid) (TCPP) as precursors to prepare dual-emitting carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) through one-step hydrothermal amide reactions ( Fig. 2 A). The CPDs were utilized for ratiometric sensing of pH in HeLa cells and imaging-guided dynamics monitoring of photodynamic therapy. Abundant amino groups anchored on the CPDs surface take protonation under acidic conditions and deprotonation under alkaline conditions, which induce the variation of FL intensities with pH changes [34]. Ding et al. prepared the hybrid semiconducting polymer dots (Pdots) through blending a block polymer (POMF, consisting of poly(ethylene glycol) methacrylate, 1-heptadecene and fluorescein o-acrylate) and a semiconducting poly(2-methoxy-5-(2-ethylhexyloxy)− 1,4-phenylenevinylene) MEHPPV to design a FRET system for ratiometric pH sensing in living cells (Fig. 2B). MEHPPV as the energy acceptor was blended with a synthesized POMF (as fluorescent pH-responsive polymer), and POMF- MEHPPV Pdots were achieved after nanoparticle reprecipitation [36]. Chen et al. prepared water- soluble semiconductor Pdots through selective re-precipitation of two/three fluorescent dyes with cross-linking reagents (Fig. 2 C). Due to the combination of both pH insensitive and sensitive dyes, the dual-emission semiconductor Pdots were prepared and used as FRNPs-pH for ratiometric pH- sensing and imaging in living cells [35].

Other types of fluorescent polymer dots are involved in the construction of FRNPs-pH systems, including ultra-bright semiconducting poly(2,5-di(30,70-dimethyloctyl)phenylene-1,4-ethynylene) Pdots for ratiometric sensing of intracellular pH [37], fluorescent organic NPs poly(3,4-dihydroxy phenylalanine) as label-free ratiometric sensors for imaging and monitoring intracellular pH [38], FL ratiometric nanosensors with a hydrophobic indicator (Ch3, Nile Blue derivative) in polymeric NPs to monitor the sub-cellular pH in endosomes and lysosomes through multiple FRETs [39]. In addition, FRNPs-pH systems have been developed based on other types of polymers. For instance, the ratiometric fluorescent pH-sensitive nanoprobes are composed of polyacrylamide NPs matrix that was covalently linked to two pH-sensitive fluorophores, which facilitate real-time intracellular measurements of pH [154]. Through covalent immobilization of pH-sensitive fluorophore with the fluorescent poly(acrylamide) NPs, a FRNPs-pH system was fabricated and utilized for ratiometric monitoring of pH inside living cells [155].

Except for FRNPs-pH systems constructed based on the use of carbon NPs, polymer dots and polymer NPs, semiconductor QDs are applied for the construction of FRNPs-pH systems. Pratiwi et al. developed single-fluorophore ratiometric pH sensors by utilizing dual-emission Mn2+-doped QDs [42]. The as-prepared Mn2+-doped CdSSe/ZnS QDs decorating with D-penicillamine (DPA- MnQdots) exhibit two emission bands at 510 nm (green) and 610 nm (red), which are respectively attributed to the exciton recombination and emission of Mn2+ dopants. A capability of ratiometric pH sensing depends on the deprotonation and protonation of carboxylic groups from DPA capping on the surface of MnQdot ( Fig. 3 A). The biocompatible DPA-MnQdots were used for long-term monitoring of local pH in HeLa cells. Bruni et al. designed a single-particle ratiometric pH probe through using dot-in-bulk CdSe/CdS nanocrystals (DiB-NCs) (Fig. 3B). The CdSe/CdS DiB-NCs exhibit two-color emissions and enable real-time monitoring of pH in living cells [44]. As for pH- sensing mechanism, H+ ions as electron scavengers result in photoluminescence (PL) decrease of the shell, while OH− species saturate the electron deficient surface states to improve PL emission intensity of the shell.

Except for the alone use of Mn2+-doped and core/shell structural semiconductor QDs with dual- emitting characteristics to explore FRNPs-pH systems, fluorescent semiconductor QDs frequently combine with organic dyes or/and other fluorophores to construct their complex systems. Under the circumstances, organic dyes and fluorophores usually act as pH-sensitive indicators, while the semiconductor QDs serve as pH-insensitive emitters and their fluorescence emissions are utilized as the reference signals to construct the dual-emitting FL ratiometric readouts [102,157–159], thus facilitating the fabrication of new FRNPs-pH systems [55,69,70,78,160].

In previous studies about FRNPs-pH systems, noble metal NCs, such as gold (AuNCs), copper (CuNCs), silver (AgNCs), platinum (PtNCs) and gold/silver bimetallic NCs (Au/Ag NCs), etc, are largely employed as functional nanomaterials to construct different types of FRNPs-pH systems. Fluorescent noble metal NCs usually combine with other luminescent nanomaterials, such as rare- earth ions-doping NPs, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or mesoporous silica (mSiO2), to yield their complex systems. Furthermore, fluorescent noble metal NCs can combine with organic dyes, fluorophores, fluorescent polymers, functional groups and moiety units to explore unique FRNPs- pH systems that contain complex compositions, particular microstructures and multiple functions towards the dual-emitting FL ratiometric modes used for in-vitro/in-vivo pH-sensing, bio-imaging and therapeutic applications [45,49,57–60,62,79].

Chandirasekar et al. developed AuNCs-loaded lysozyme nanoparticles (LysNPs) for label-free ratiometric fluorescence sensing of pH ( Fig. 4 A). The aggregated lysozyme-stabilized Lys-AuNCs were treated with glutaraldehyde, to trigger a conversion of the aggregated lysozymes into green- emitting lys-NPs via forming CN bonds. Enzymatic reaction causes pH changes and pH-induced variation of LysNPs’ FL, which allows Lys-AuNCs to develop toward a FRNPs-pH system for the monitoring of pH in live cells [59]. Cao et al. prepared poly(allylamine hydrochloride)-stabilized red-emitting PAH-AuNCs, followed by assembly with pH-sensitive pyranine (HPTS) to develop cell-permeable HPTS-PAH-AuNCs nanoprobes (Fig. 4B). The nanoprobes possess dual-emission bands, acid pH-sensitive AuNCs and basic pH-sensitive HPTS. These imply potential applications of the nanoprobes in ratiometric sensing and imaging of intracellular pH [62].

Additionally, cerium/gold (Ce/Au) bimetallic NCs [45], Cu-based MOFs-derived CuNCs [49], AuNC@FITC@fluorophores inorganic/organic hybrid [57], folic acid-FITC@AuNC system [58], FITC-cationic peptides (CP)/CP@AuNCs ternary nanoplatform [60], Au/Ag bimetallic NCs [63] and AuNC@FITC-mSiO2 @RhB-copolymer [79] were largely utilized to construct different types of FRNPs-pH systems for bio-applications in ratiometric bio-sensing, bio-imaging and monitoring of pH at the in-vitro/vivo levels. Among them, pH-sensitive emission sources are from noble metal NCs, organic dyes, fluorophore molecules and other fluorescent groups in the specific structures of FRNPs-pH systems. In other words, therein both noble metal NCs and organic fluorophores can serve as the reference and emit pH-insensitive FL, showing different dual-emission characteristics. This unique variability of pH-sensitive emitters can endow the noble metal NCs-based FRNPs-pH systems with flexible designs of dual-emitting ratiometric readouts. By contrast, previous studies abundantly involve the construction of dual-emitting FL ratiometric nanoprobes for various targets beyond pH, originating from efficient combination of noble metal NCs with luminescent inorganic nanomaterials, organic fluorophores or inorganic-organic complex systems [161–165].

Except for fluorescent nanomaterials mentioned above, other types of functional nanomaterials are used to construct FRNPs-pH systems, including modified biomass QDs [43], Eu-doped silicon nanorods [46], [EuL3] complex [47], Zr(IV)-doped MOFs [48], Ti3C2 QDs-[Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 complex [55], modified Eu3+/Tb3+ mixture [56], FITC-modified silicon nanodots [64], 0D silicon nanodots- aptamer cross-linking fluorescein [65], cyclometalated Ir(III) complex coupled to fluorescein [66], Pt(II)-porphyrin complex [84], etc. Typically, Ding et al. explored the self-assembly of nanoscale MOFs (energy acceptors) and pH-responsive polymers (donors) for ratiometric fluorometric pH sensing [48]. The light-harvesting nanoscale MOFs include Zr(IV) and π-conjugated dicarboxylate ligands, and the fluorophores contain cyaine dyes and a Bodipy dye. This self-assembled complex forms a FRET nanosystem ( Fig. 5 A). Local pH variation triggers the changes of stimuli-respective polymer chain lengths and corresponding FL changes of polymers. The nanoprobes with superior water dispersibility and cell membrane permeability were used for imaging of pH in HeLa cells. Liu et al. developed a ratiometric time-resolved luminescence sensor of pH based on lanthanide (Eu3+, Tb3+) complexes with terpyridine polyacid derivative (HTTA) [56]. In the weakly acidic-to -neutral media, HTTA-Eu3+ is strongly pH-sensitive, but HTTA-Tb3+ is pH-independent (Fig. 5B). The properties allow HTTA-Eu3+/Tb3+ mixture to be used for ratiometric luminescence sensing of pH and time-resolved luminescence imaging of cells.

Biomass QDs were prepared by Osmanthus leaves with folic acid and polyoxyethylene diamine as modifiers [43]. One characteristic emission of dual-emitting biomass QDs exhibits pH-sensitive changes, but the other is pH-insensitive. Biomass QDs were used as a multifunctional nanoprobe for the monitoring of mitochondrion pH changes in cancer cells via ratiometric FL imaging. As for Eu(III)-doped 1D silicon nanorods (Eu@SiNRs), the pH increase causes a significantly decreased emission from silicon nanorods, but negligible effects on Eu(III) emission [46]. Eu@SiNRs were explored for ratiometric imaging detection of intracellular pH in live cells. Lanthanide complexes, such as [EuL3] [47] and [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 [55], usually consist of the coordinated organic sensitizers, pH-dependent organic dye moieties and rare-earth metal ions to provide multiple emissions. These unique composition facilitates flexible design of dual-emission ratiometric modes for the imaging of intracellular pH. By conjugating Ti3C2 QDs and [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 [55], the surface-functionalized complex shows a highly pH-sensitive PL of Ti3C2 QDs and a pH-insensitive PL of [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2. The complex-based ratiometric sensor shows a capability to monitor intracellular pH.

After covalent modification of NH2-terminated 0D silicon nanodots (SiNDs) with pH-sensitive FITC, the prepared SiNDs-FITC material exhibits high performance in ratiometric pH-sensing and cellular imaging applications [64]. pH-sensitive fluorescein tagged aptamer was used to covalently modify SiNDs to explore a ratiometric pH aptasensor [65]. The SiNDs-featured biosensor enables lysosomal imaging and pH detection. To construct FRNPs-pH systems, the rare-earth metal ions are doped in fluorescent nanomaterials (SiNRs, MOFs) to form functionalized nanoprobe systems. By contrast, lanthanide complexes not only can be directly utilized as FRNPs-pH systems ([EuL3], Eu3+/Tb3+ mixture), but also can conjugate with fluorescent nanomaterials (Ti3C2 QDs) or couple to organic dyes (fluorescein) to produce unique FRNPs-pH systems [66,166]. pH-sensitive organic dyes were covalently attached to NH2-cellulose or polyacrylonitrile particles, and Pt(II)-porphyrin complex was incorporated into polystyrene particles. The composites of polymer particles exhibit three emissions and can be explored as a sensor film through RGB readout techniques [84]. The composites-based fluorescent sensor film realizes the ratiometric imaging and in-vivo monitoring of pH spatial distribution on the plain skin surface of volar forearm.

Except for the alone use of fluorescent nanomaterials to construct FRNPs-pH systems, previous studies largely involve different preparation methods to explore various inorganic/organic hybrids- based FRNPs-pH systems towards the in-vitro/vivo pH-sensing, bio-imaging and bio-applications, principally referring to inorganic/organic conjugates, core/shell structural systems, composites and mixture systems, assembled functional materials, etc. Adjustable hybrid compositions, particular microstructures and multiple functions can endow the inorganic/organic hybrids-based FRNPs-pH systems with great prospects, especially in in-vitro/in-vivo sensing of pH, imaging and therapeutic applications. In the following sections, we introduce different construction methods for inorganic/ organic hybrids-based FRNPs-pH systems, and rationally discuss the each construction method by analyzing representative and state-of-the-art related studies (Table 2).

Fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials often combine with organic dyes and other fluorophores to construct the inorganic/organic conjugates-based FRNPs-pH systems through surface conjugation reactions of organic groups from dissociative fluorescent molecules with surface ligands or active functional groups on the surface of inorganic nanomaterials. As for the resulting inorganic/organic conjugates, pH-sensitive luminescence responses can come from either inorganic nanomaterials or the conjugated organic molecules. One of them serves as the reference signal, while the other acts as the response signal. Moreover, pH-sensitive luminescence responses can be derived from both of them based on specific luminescence mechanisms (such as FRET) to facilitate the fabrication of dual-dependant luminescence ratiometric readouts. Du et al. designed a ratiometric pH nanosensor via covalent linking of pH-sensitive fluorescent dyes (FITC) onto CDs [51]. In the nanoplatform, CDs as energy donor well match with FITC as the acceptor. pH changes trigger the structural and spectral conversion of FITC moieties onto CDs, which affords a FRET-based pH sensor ( Fig. 6 A). The CDs-FITC conjugates were used for intracellular pH-sensing and mapping.

Lin et al. reported a ratiometric nanoprobe with single-excitation dual emissions [53]. N-doped CQDs were linked with fluorescent porphyrin derivative of meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (H2TCPP). pH-dependant near-infrared FL of H2TCPP serves as the response signal, but the FL of N-CQDs is pH-insensitive and acts as internal reference signal. This near-infrared FL ratiometric nanoprobe was used for real-time tracing intracellular pH changes during apoptosis by ratiometric FL imaging (Fig. 6B). Zhang et al. prepared FITC-modified SiNDs for ratiometric pH-sensing and cellular FL imaging [64]. The NH2-terminated SiNDs were modified with pH-sensitive fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) via a covalent licking method. After covalent conjugation of FITC onto the surface of pH-insensitive SiNDs, the conjugates show superior anti-interference ability and enable ratiometric pH sensing in human breast cancer MCF-7 cells. The label-free fluorescent SiNDs can be treated by the cross-linking covalent modification with pH-sensitive fluorescein tagged aptamer (Apt-FAM) [65]. This SiNDs-Apt-FAM featured ratiometric aptasensor enables the utilization for lysosomal imaging and pH sensing in MCF-7 cells.

Notably, some fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials directly combine with organic dyes or other fluorophores to construct the inorganic/organic hybrid-based FRNPs-pH systems without covalent cross-linking and chemical conjugation reactions between functional groups of fluorescent organic molecules and surface ligands (or active functional groups) on the surface of fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials [42,50,55]. Moreover, two or more types of organic dyes (or other fluorophores) are chemically conjugated or covalently linked with inorganic nanomaterials to prepare the inorganic/ organic conjugates. However, inorganic nanomaterials only act as the substrate, carrier or support materials of organic fluorophores, but not serve as the reference or response signal to design dual- emission ratiometric readout [52]. These as-mentioned particular types are not within the scope of this section, and they will be further discussed in following sections.

Previous studies have widely referred to the construction strategies of FRNPs-pH systems based on core/shell structural systems. Commonly, functionally modified inorganic nanomaterials (such as AuNCs, QDs, Si-NPs, mSiO2, NPs, etc.) serve as the core, while organic or polymer molecules (dyes, fluorophores, peptides, copolymer, gels, etc.) act as the shell. Besides, the core/shell/shelled systems are involved, and contain functional inorganic nanomaterials as the core layers. Complex dual/multi-shelled layers majorly consist of modified inorganic nanomaterials, organic molecules, polymers, or multiple complex compositions. Fu et al. developed a core-shell silica nanoprobe for ratiometric sensing of pH in live cells [74]. pH-independent red-FL dye (RhB as reference signal) was embedded in silica core to form RhB-doped fluorescent Si-NPs. Then, pH-sensitive green-FL dye (FITC as response signal) was grafted onto the outer surface of NH2-coated pure silica shell to form silica nanospheres. Yang et al. explored a dual-ratiometric nanoprobe for visually monitoring dynamic process of pH changes in autophagy and apoptosis [75]. The nanoprobe consists of RhB- loaded mSiO2 core, pH/O2 •−-sensitive fluorescent molecule probes (Tpy-Cy/DBZTC) doped in the outer layer shell of SiO2, chitosan and triphenylphosphonium modified on the surface of SiO2 ( Fig. 7 A). The nanoprobe can visually monitor the changes of mitochondrial O2 •− and pH in HeLa cells through dual-emission ratiometric intracellular imaging [78].

Wang et al. developed a cell-penetrating Janus nanoprobe for ratiometric FL detection of pH in living cells [76]. Silica acts as raw material core. One side of SiO2-NH2 NPs was partially locked in the wax and modified with FITC. The exposed half of Janus NPs reacted with modified RhB. Modified RhB and FITC probes were individually encapsulated in the independent hemispheres of Janus microparticles prepared via Pickering emulsion (Fig. 7B), so as to obtain a unique core-shell structural ratiometric nanoprobe [77]. Shi et al. explored a theranostic nanomedicine consisting of a ratiometric pH sensor combining with functional gold nanocage (AuNC) [79]. AuNC was coated with FITC-doped fluorescent mSiO2. Then, thermo-sensitive copolymer containing pH-switchable RhB monomer was grafted onto the channel and outer shell of mSiO2 scafford. The resulting core/ shell functional system is able to efficiently determine and trace intracellular pH changes through ratiometric FL sensing and imaging. Lee et al. developed core-shell aluminosilicate (SiO4/Al) NPs that covalently encapsulate a reference dye in the core. A pH-sensitive dye moiety was introduced into the shell through the conjugation of functional groups from surface polymer linker (stabilizer, capping reagent) and dye moiety [80]. The modified core-shell NPs show an enhanced application performance in super-resolution live-cell ratiometric pH sensing.

Besides, Yao et al. adopted a co-precipitation strategy to develop the core/shell/shelled systems- based FRNPs-pH [167]. Porphyrin complex was introduced into a hydrophobic core through basic hydrolysis of co-polymers (as a protective agent shell layer of the formed polystyrene NPs). FITC- doped poly-L-lysine serves as the outer shell coating on the inner shell to produce FITC/porphyrin dual-doped fluorescent polymer NPs. Due to pH-insensitive porphyrin complex (reference signal) doped in the core and pH-sensitive FITC (response signal) doped in the outer shell, the core/shell/ shelled NPs were explored as FRNPs-pH toward ratiometric pH-sensing and intracellular imaging. Particularly, 2D structural core/shell systems were explored as unique FRNPs-pH. Both FITC and Nile dyes were conjugated onto the substrate surface of CDs to form CDs@FITC-Nile fluorescent probes of pH [83]. Then, the probes were subsequently immobilized into sodium alginate hydrogel to prevent the leakage of particles. As a result, CDs@FITC-Nile@Gel was achieved and explored as 2D core/shell system-based FRNPs-pH for applications in pH-sensing and real-time tracking of cell proliferation and metabolism.

In this section, FRNPs-pH systems constructed based on various dual-emission composites and mixture systems are discussed in detail. Unlike fluorescent inorganic/organic conjugates and core/ shell structural systems mentioned above, the composites or mixture systems are usually prepared by weak intermolecular interactions, including electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, Van der Waals force, adsorption, etc., rather than covalent cross-linking coupling or chemical conjugation reactions. Dual-emitting nanomaterial systems are often modified with functional polymers or bio- macromolecules, serving as surface stabilizing or capping reagents of nanomaterials. Two types of organic dyes or other fluorophores can be attached onto the surface of non-luminescent inorganic nanomaterials to develop dual-emitting composites. In addition, two types of fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials, organic dyes and other fluorophores can be directly mixed to explore dual-emitting mixture systems. Previous studies largely refer to the construction methods of FRNPs-pH systems based on dual-emitting composites or mixture systems [27,42,45,50,56,84].

Pal et al. synthesized B-doped N,S carbon dots (B-Cdots) and organic luminescent carbon dots (Cdotsorange) [27]. The nanocomposites of Cdots and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were prepared via adding Cdotsorange solution to PVA solution under stirring, followed by adding B-Cdots solution to the final mixture. Then, Cdots were encapsulated inside the polymer matrix to prepare Cdots/PVA nanocomposites ( Fig. 8 A). pH variation induces effective surface modification due to protonation- deprotonation equilibrium of surface functional groups on Cdots, so causing significant changes in dual-emission of Cdots. The Cdots/PVA nanocomposites were utilized for ratiometric intracellular pH sensing. Similarly, Ce/Au NCs were prepared by using Ce(IV) and Au(III) ions in the presence of bovine serum albumin (BSA, as surface stabilizer) under alkaline conditions [45]. BSA-Ce/Au NCs show pH-dependent emission at 410 nm (BSA-Ce complex) and pH-independent emission at 650 nm (Au NCs), and can be utilized as the ratiometric pH nanoprobes for imaging determination of local pH inside HeLa cells. Dual-emitting Mn2+-doped QDs were decorated with DPA through ligand exchange [42]. pH-sensitive ligand (DPA) and active photophysical properties of MnQdots facilitate the achievement of a single-fluorophore ratiometric pH sensor based on DPA-MnQdots, and its application for the monitoring of local pH in living cells (Fig. 3 A).

He et al. prepared CDs-organic molecules nanohybrid through electrostatic interactions between CDs and organic dyes [50]. The dual-FL nanohybrid contains pH-sensitive dye (1,8-naphthalimide derivatives) and pH-independent CDs, and can be explored as a hybrid nanosensor for ratiometric fluorescent detection of acidic pH in lysosome (Fig. 8B). Liu et al. prepared the mixture of HTTA- Eu3+ and HTTA-Tb3+ complexes [56]. Due to pH-dependent deprotonation of 4′-hydroxy groups from HTTA, different responses of Tb3+ and Tb3+ emissions to pH variations are conducive to the fabrication of a ratiometric luminescent sensor for pH-sensing and cell imaging (Fig. 5B). Meier et al. explored a sensor film that contains three immobilized luminescent indicator probes, including pH-sensitive FITC, oxygen-responsive Pt(II)-porphyrin complex and a reference fluorophore DPA [84]. FITC and DPA were respectively attached on amino-cellulose particles and polyacrylonitrile particles. Pt(II)-porphyrin complex was incorporated into polystyrene particles. The composites of polymer particles have three emissions in red/green/blue channels. Through using a RGB readout technique, the composites were used to construct a ratiometric sensor film for ratiometric imaging and in-vivo monitoring of pH spatial distribution.

In previous studies, various assembled functional materials are largely explored to construct the FRNPs-pH systems. The assembled functional materials often consist of inorganic nanomaterials, biomacromolecules, polymers, organic dyes, fluorophores, surface ligands, functional molecules, inorganic/organic hybrids, etc. In this section, at least three types of components are used to yield the dual-emitting assembled functional materials via layer-by-layer assembly, weak intermolecular interactions (electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, Van der Waals force, adsorption, etc.), or chemical reactions (covalent cross-linking, functional group coupling, specific conjugation, etc.). The pH-sensitive FITC and organic fluorophores were simultaneously conjugated to the surface of organic ligands-capping fluorescent AuNCs [57]. The AuNCs-based inorganic-organic nanoprobe can monitor pH in living cells via ratiometric multicolor imaging. Similarly, thiolated anthracene (PET-based pH ligand) molecules and thiolated RhB ligands were self-assembled onto the surface of AuNPs to develop a ratiometric PET-based nanosensor for intracellular pH measurements [61]. A ternary nanoplatform was constructed by non-covalent combination of BSA-protected AuNCs, FITC-free cationic peptides (FPen) and FITC-labeled FPen [60]. Red fluorescent AuNCs serve as the reference. FITC-labeled FPen is used for specific H+ recognition. FITC-unlabeled FPen acts as the delivery ( Fig. 9 A). The FITC-FPen/FPen@AuNCs assembles were explored as a cell-surface- specific ratiometric fluorescent probe for extracellular pH sensing.

Cao et al. adopted a charge neutralization strategy to conduct efficient assembly of AuNCs with functional components [62]. Cationic polymer (PAH) was chemically conjugated to the surface of AuNCs, followed by self-assembly for negatively charged pH-sensitive fluorophores (HPTS) with PAH-AuNCs through electrostatic attraction (Fig. 4B). The developed HPTS-PAH-AuNCs system enables ratiometric sensing and imaging of intracellular pH. Except for noble metal NCs and NPs, other inorganic nanomaterials (CDs, polymer NPs/dots, Fe3O4 NPs, graphene oxide, etc.) are used as the substrates (carrier, support materials) to perform functional self-assembly of the substrates with fluorophores or/and auxiliary components [82,168,169].
