A pH-sensitive moiety, consisting of non-fluorescent pH indicator, aniline blue and pH-insensitive RhB, combines with pH-insensitive CDs prepared from pyrolysis of citric acid and 1,4-butanediamine [170]. CDs-based systems were applied as a dual-emission ratiometric pH probe for intracellular pH detection [83]. A pH-sensitive dye (ANNA) was linked by using a peptide substrate of the matrix (metalloprotease-9) with Fe3O4 NPs [68]. After the conjugation of ANNA dye-labeled peptide with PEGylated Fe3O4 NPs and the conjugation of folic acid with peptide-modified Fe3O4 NPs, a FRET system was prepared and used for pH sensing in tumor micro-environment.

Red-emitting Nile Red was encapsulated in polystyrene (PS) NPs to prepare pH-inert dye [171], followed by surface coupling of PS-NPs with pH-responsive dye FITC to produce a dual-emitting polymeric nanosensor for ratiometric measurements of pH in biofilms. Typically, SiO2 and mSiO2 NPs are largely mentioned during the preparation process of assembled functional materials. RhB dye was encapsulated into silica NPs. Then, FITC was grafted onto the surface of fluorescent core- shell silica colloidal particles (RhB-FSiNPs) to develop a RhB-FSiNPs-FITC probe for ratiometric FL detection of pH in living cells [74]. Semiconductor QDs were encapsulated into a silica shell to form core-shelled SiO2 @QDs that act as the carrier to assemble pH-sensitive dye FITC and O2 •−- responsive fluorophore hydroethidine (HE). After conjugation of mitochondria-targeted molecules (TPP) onto NH2-terminated SiO2 @QDs, the assembled system “HE+FITC+TPP-SiO2 @QDs” was developed toward a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for biosensing and imaging of intracellular pH [78]. As for the use of mSiO2, Au nanocage was coated with FITC-doped mSiO2, and thermo- sensitive copolymer was grafted onto the channel and outer shell of the mSiO2 scafford to develop complex assembled systems [79]. Two types of dyes were co-condensed with a silica precursor to get dual-dye encapsulated mSiO2 NPs, followed by modification with charged silane and template removing to form functional fluorophores attaching on an interior surface [72]. The functionalized mSiO2 NPs realize fluorescent ratiometric imaging of intracellular pH (Fig. 9B). RhB was loaded into mSiO2 core and pH/O2 •−-sensitive fluorescent molecules were doped in the shell layer of SiO2 [75]. Then, chitosan and triphenylphosphonium were modified onto the outer surface of SiO2 (Fig. 7 A). The layer-by-layer assembles serve as the ratiometric nanoprobe for visual monitoring of the changes of mitochondrial pH in HeLa cells.

In the construction process of FRNPs-pH systems, except for the types mentioned above, other types of inorganic/organic hybrids are occasionally involved, majorly including nanoscale metal- organic layers [172], ratiometric fluorescent microgels [173], assembled core/shell 2D hydrogels [83], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) active polyheterocycles [174], AIE active two-channel ratiometric fluorogen [175], triple-labeled polyelectrolyte microcapsules [176], Y3Al5O12:V3+,V5+ NPs [177], dual-fluorescent hollow silica nanofibers [178], etc. FITC and Nile blue chloride dyes were conjugated to the surface of CDs through chemical coupling between functional groups [83]. The CDs@Nile-FITC probe was entrapped in sodium alginate gel to form a 2D ratiometric FL pH sensor for cell imaging. Triply emissive nanoscale metal-organic layers (nMOL) were explored by covalent conjugation of pH-sensitive/independent dyes (FITC/RhB, or F/R) to BTB-NH2 ligands. Zr-BTB nMOL was functionalized to produce NA@Zr-BTB/F/R via exchanging formate capping groups on Zr6 secondary building units with glutathione-selective organic fluorophore (NA) ( Fig. 10 A). The NA@Zr-BTB/F/R nMOL was used for ratiometric pH-sensing and cell imaging [172]. The dual-fluorescent hollow silica nanofibers (hSNFs) and an optical fiber detection system were used to develop a ratiometric pH-sensing system (Fig. 10B). FITC and Ru(BPY)3 were doped into hSNFs via two-step operations in a reverse micelle system. The hSNFs enable in-situ monitoring of pH in cell culture media [178].

pH-sensitive BSA NPs and pH-insensitive Nile red were loaded into polyurethane via emulsion solvent evaporation to fabricate ratiometric fluorescent microgels for pH sensing in extracellular microenvironment [173]. Two pH-sensitive dyes (FITC, oregon green) and one pH-insensitive dye RhB were simultaneously labeled onto microcapsules [176]. Triple-polyelectrolyte microcapsules were applied for the localized ratiometric sensing of intracellular pH and real-time cell imaging. In addition, the spatial segregation of vanadium ions (V3+, V5+) at differential oxidation states within Y3Al5O12 NPs induces different spectroscopic properties, in sensitively responding to pH variation of the medium [177]. As for Y3Al5O12:V3+,V5+ NPs, the emission intensity of V5+ localized in the surface part of NCs is much efficiently quenched by the vibrational modes of –OH groups, when compared to that of V3+ localized in the inner part of NCs. The emission intensity ratios (V5+/V3+) are monotonically pH-dependent in a wide range of pH 2–11, exhibiting a high relative sensitivity for pH detection. Although previous researches referring to some inorganic/organic hybrids-based FRNPs-pH systems, do not involve biomedical sensing and imaging applications [49,143,160,166,171,169,173,177–181], superior pH-sensing performances, high biocompatibility, flexible and versatile features can endow them with great prospects for promising in-vitro/in-vivo sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications.

Previous studies about FRNPs-pH systems reveal many promising in-vitro/in-vivo applications, especially in bio-sensing, bio-imaging and therapeutic fields. In subsequent sections, we rationally analyze and discuss various biomedical applications of FRNPs-pH systems by highlighting typical studies published during the past one decade. The subsequent sections mainly refer to the imaging of living cells and small animals, pH-sensing and imaging detection in entire cells and organelles, pH-sensing and imaging detection in tissues and organs, therapeutic applications, bio-toxicity and bio-distribution, etc. The details are available as shown as below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of living cells and small animals
ce:section.ce:para: As briefly summarized in Table 1 and Table 2, the previously reported FRNPs-pH systems have been widely used for imaging studies of living cells, such as U251N, MCF-7, HeLa, SMMC-7721, MDA-MB-231, SK-BR-3, 293 T, T24, 7702, HEK-293, NIH-3T3, L929, HepG2, HUVE, A549, CHO, DND-189, MCF-10A, LS180, BEAS-2B, RAW264.7 living cells, etc. Herein, the following section majorly introduces various imaging studies about FRNPs-pH systems at the level of living cells [22,30,35,37,40,43,57,58,65,70,72,74], but it dose not highlight the FL imaging detection of intracellular pH (as shown in the Section 5.2). For a better introduction, this section is divided into two principal parts, including FRNPs-pH systems used for conventional intracellular imaging and real-time intracellular imaging. For conventional intracellular imaging of FRNPs-pH, specifically, dual-emitting CDs were prepared through solvothermal treatment of cabbage [30]. Green emission shows reversible response to pH due to deprotonation/protonation of surface functional groups and non-covalent interactions. Except for unique dual-emitting features of CDs developed as label-free ratiometric fluorometric probes, the CDs demonstrate low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility, and enable in-vitro cellular uptake into HeLa cells for bioimaging of living cells, showing superior cell imaging capability in green/red channels ( Fig. 11 A).

As for real-time intracellular imaging of FRNPs-pH systems [21,24,39,42,44,46,47,49,51,53,56,68,75,80,81,83], notably, a hydrogel sensor (CDs@Nile-FITC@Gel) was prepared by conjugation and immobilization procedures [83]. The resulting 2D ratiometric fluorescent sensor was used for real-time monitoring of intracellular pH changes. After HeLa cells incubated with the pH-sensitive sensor, pH variation in the process of cell proliferation and metabolism was tracked by real-time ratiometric FL imaging (Fig. 11B). With extending of cell proliferation and metabolism, green-FL gradually reduces, while red-FL is almost unchanged. FL intensity ratio of the green/red channels obviously decreases with extending time of cell proliferation and metabolism. These results imply that the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor enables real-time tracking of time-dependent pH variations during cell proliferation and metabolism through ratiometric FL imaging of cells, which reveal great prospects in therapeutic applications.

Previous studies have widely referred to imaging applications of FRNPs-pH systems at the level of small animals and organisms [31,33,49,67,68,79,81], such as E. coli, L. Casei, zebrafish, mice, etc. Dual-emitting CDs were prepared via hydrothermal carbonization of o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid [31]. The CDs show pH-sensitive red FL and pH-insensitive blue FL, and enable ratiometric FL detection of intracellular pH. The CDs were utilized to label E. coli in blue and red channels ( Fig. 12 A). FL intensity of E. coli incubated with the CDs in culture medium at pH 2.5 is higher than that at pH 7.4 in red channels, while FL intensity is nearly identical in blue channels. These results indicate that the CDs can be used for FL imaging of pH variations inside E. coli. By hydrothermal treatments of neutral red and urea, CDs were prepared and produced dual-emission bands [33]. FL band at 542 nm is much sensitive to pH, while that at 440 nm is almost unchanged. The CDs were applied for ratiometric FL sensing of pH in HeLa cells. Especially, the CDs serving as biolabeling agents in living animals were used to conduct imaging of zebrafish (Fig. 12B). The CDs were rapidly adsorbed in the zebrafish via the chorion and mouth. FL signals concentrate in the digestive system and spread over zebrafish body (tissues, organs, even skeletal structure, etc.). By the conjugation of an ANNA-labeled peptide with PEGylated Fe3O4 NPs and the conjugation of folic acid with the peptide-modified Fe3O4 NPs, a FRET-based probe was explored and used for pH sensing in vivo [68]. After intravenous delivery of the probe through the tail vein of tumor- bearing mice, the multi-modality imaging of LS180 tumor xenografts were conducted (Fig. 12 C). FL signals at Cy5.5/ANNA channels are observable at tumor sites, and display the time-dependent changes in FL brightness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensing and imaging detection in entire cells and organelles
ce:section.ce:para: One of significant in-vitro applications for FRNPs-pH systems refers to fluorescent pH-sensing and imaging detection of pH at the level of entire cells and organelles. Numerous relevant studies about FRNPs-pH systems have extensively reported “FL imaging-assisted sensing of intracellular pH” and “pH-tunable FL imaging of living cells” (Tables 1,2). Typically, BSA-Ce/Au NCs with pH-dependent emission at 410 nm and pH-independent emission at 650 nm were used to construct ratiometric pH probes [45]. HeLa cells were incubated with the NCs in TBS solutions at different pH values. Upon pH decline, blue cell images became weaker, while red ones are almost constant ( Fig. 13 A). As observed in FL images, the NCs were located in the cytosol inside HeLa cells. The ratios of blue-to-red cell images indicate purple cell images. These results reveal the advantages of BSA-Ce/Au NCs for monitoring local pH inside cells. Liu et al. reported pH-responsive CDs-pH for FL ratiometric imaging of living cells [24]. By cultivation of HeLa cells with CDs-pH in cell culture medium (pH, adjusted from 5.0 to 8.0), relevant confocal FL images were measured (Fig. 13B). Distinct pseudo-color changes are observed in a physiological pH range, exhibiting superior performance for intracellular pH imaging. CDs-based ratiometric FL nanoprobes of pH are largely used for sensing and imaging detection of intracellular pH [25]. HeLa cells were incubated with CDs in PBS buffer. Intracellular pH was fixed by treatments with high-K+ HEPES-buffer solution in the presence of nigericin. FL ratio (I blue/I yellow) images were recorded by using Image-Pro Plus software to calculate the average FL intensities of channel 1 and channel 2 (Fig. 13 C). As testified, pH-sensitive dual-emitting CDs can perform both ratiometric pH-sensing and ratiometric imaging- detection of physiological pH levels inside living cells.

Besides, FRNPs-pH systems are applied to ratiometric FL pH-sensing and imaging detection of pH at the level of organelles [21,39,43,46,50,54,65,71,73,78,80], such as lysosomes, mitochondria (cytochondria), cytoplasm, cell nuclei, etc. FRNPs-pH often serve as active bio-labeling agents to perform the organelles-targeting sensing of intracellular pH and localization imaging detection of pH inside living cells. To reveal the organelles-targeting capability of FRNPs-pH via FL imaging technique, different organelles-tracker dyes (MitoTracker: rhodamine 123, green MTG, deep red FM; LysoTracker: blue DND-22, red DND-99, DND-26, Cys deep red, etc.) are usually adopted to perform Mito/Lyso-targeting intracellular imaging. Imaging results from the use of Mito/Lyso- Trackers (as reference) are compared with those from direct use of FRNPs-pH during the process of organelles-targeting images. Typically, fluorescent Eu@SiNRs-based pH sensors were used for the ratiometric measurement of cytoplasmic pH in live cells [46]. Eu@SiNRs can enter into cells through energy-dependent endocytosis and temperature-dependent cellular internalization process. Remarkable blue/red FL signals of Eu@SiNRs are detectable in the cytoplasm when the cells are incubated with Eu@SiNRs at 37 °C for 12 h ( Fig. 14). Eu@SiNRs realize ratiometric intracellular pH-sensing and imaging detection of cytoplasmic pH. In addition, the ratiometric FL nanoprobes are composed of mSiO2 assembled with amino-fluorescein as recognition unit of pH and ethidium bromide as reference fluorophore, followed by conjugation of sub-cellular targeting moieties that enable the nanoprobes to specifically target lysosomes and mitochondria [71]. Two targeting dyes (MitoTracker Green, LysoTracker DND-26) were used to co-stain with the nanoprobes in MCF-7 cells. As testified by confocal FL cell images, the nanoprobes possess the capability to ratiometric imaging detection of pH in lysosomes and mitochondria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensing and imaging detection in tissues and organs
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of in-vivo applications, the FRNPs-pH systems can be utilized for ratiometric FL pH- sensing and imaging detection of pH at the level of tissues and organs. Specifically, blue/yellow dual-emitting CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatments of neutral red and urea, and used for FL imaging of zebrafish [33]. With pH raise, stronger yellow FL signal appears in zebrafish, while blue FL signal has negligible changes. FL signals majorly concentrate in the digestive system and spread over the zebrafish body, including tissues, organs and skeletal structures (Fig. 12B). These images indicate superior ratiometric FL imaging performance of pH-dependent dual-emitting CDs at the in-vivo level. A target-triggered fluorescent nanoprobe was prepared by linking Cy5.5 dyes with Fe3O4 NPs based on a peptide substrate of matrix MMP-9 [68]. Upon cleavage of the peptide linker by MMP-9, FL of ANNA is activated and displays an “ON” state, which can be applied for in-vivo pH mapping of tumor xenografts. In the peptide substrate, both ANNA dye (pH-sensitive) and folic acid (folate receptor-targeting in cancerous tissues) are labeled before cleavage. A FRET from ANNA to Cy5.5 happens due to protonation/deprotonation-induced structural transformation of ANNA, and enables ratiometric FL pH-sensing and pH mapping in tumor sites and other organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, intestine, bone, muscle) of tumor-bearing mice.

To develop a protease-activated ratiometric FL nanoprobe, the surface capping ligands of Fe3O4 NPs were covalently attached to anti-tumor antibody via a peptide substrate of matrix MMP-9 [67]. The peptide substrate serves as a linker of particle quencher and chromophore, and is labeled with anti-tumor antibody and pH-sensitive ANNA dye (off-state). Due to cleavage of the peptide linker by protease, FL of ANNA dye is recovered (on-state). Based on ratiometric pH responses to tumor microenvironment, the nanoprobe was applied for protease-activated FL imaging of subcutaneous tumor xenografts to realize the real-time pH-mapping monitoring of malignant tumors. AuNC@F- mSiO2 @R-copolymer was explored as a ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor, and utilized for in-vivo and ex-vivo imaging of normal and tumor-bearing mouse [79]. By comparing FL imaging signals from different organs (heart, spleen, liver, lung, kidney, tumor sites), the sensor emits green FL at physiological conditions, but emits pH-activated red FL in acidic tumor microenvironment. A pH- sensitive near-infrared FL probe (SiRB) was prepared by introducing boronic acid groups into Si- rhodamine structures [81]. Indocyanine green (ICG, as reference) and SiRB were loaded into poly lactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA) to form PLGA-SiRB-ICG NPs. In acidic environment, PLGA can accelerate the release of cleavage, and FL ratios of two probes reflect specific pH values in tumors ( Fig. 15). Based on pH-responsive ex-vivo FL images of major organs (liver, spleen, kidney, heart, lung) and tumors dissected from mice, PLGA-SiRB-ICG NPs realize quantitative pH-detection in tumor sites, and real-time ex-vivo FL imaging and ratiometric FL tracking of tumors with different sizes collected from tumor-bearing mice. The results reveal great prospects of FRNPs-pH systems in the application process of tumor diagnosis and treatment.

Except for superior performances in pH-sensing and imaging-detection applications in vitro and in vivo, the previously reported FRNPs-pH systems can act as advanced functional nanomaterials with versatile biomedical characteristics for in-vivo therapeutic applications. Specifically, Shi et al. explored a smart “sense-act-treat” system that was prepared by combining a ratiometric pH sensor with a near-infrared (NIR) therapeutic gold nanocage (AuNC) [79]. The multifunctional AuNC@ F-mSiO2 @R-copolymer ratiometric FL nanodevice was utilized for confocal microscopy imaging in HeLa cells clamped at different pH conditions. Due to photothermal effects of AuNC, thermo- sensitive and pH-switchable smart R-copolymer, the nanodevice performed photothermal ablation therapy at tumor sites of H22 tumor-bearing mice under irradiation using a NIR laser (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2). At higher laser intensity, the photothermal effects of AuNC@F-mSiO2 @R-copolymer are sufficient to inhibit tumor growth. Cheng et al. developed a ratiometric theranostic nanoprobe for pH imaging-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT) [182]. The nanoprobe contains hydrophilic PEG with the linking of a FRET fluorophore pair (RhB as the donor, photosensitizer protoporphyrin IX PpIX as the acceptor), followed by assembly into micelles ( Fig. 16 A). This prepared RhB-PEG8- K(PpIX) nanoprobe (RPP) shows ratiometric FL responses to pH changes in live cells. In addition, RPP can generate 1O2 under light irradiation in time-/pH-dependent manners.

RPP realized ratiometric pH-sensing and imaging detection through CLSM images of 4T1 cells at different pH values. pH-related 1O2 production by RPP was studied in vitro (Fig. 16B). Guided by ratiometric pH sensing of RPR, the PDT therapeutic effects from PpIX against 4T1 cells were evaluated to reveal the anti-tumor capability of RPP under different pH conditions (Fig. 16 C). As confirmed, the RPP nanoprobe exhibits pH-dependent PDT therapeutic effects in vitro/in vivo, and can be applied for ratiometric pH imaging-guided PDT against tumor cells. RPP-induced PDT can effectively restrain the growth and metastasis of 4T1 tumor with low side effects in vivo. Hu et al. designed dual-emission carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) for ratiometric pH sensing of HeLa cells (Fig. 2 A). CPDs consisting of carbonized PEI units (417 nm blue FL) and TCPP (665 nm red-FL), exhibit pH-sensitive dual emissions. Due to pH-dependent generation of 1O2, CPDs were used for imaging-guided dynamics monitoring of PDT [34]. As testified, CPDs can produce 1O2 under light irradiation (1 W/cm2) to induce the deformation of tumor cells, and achieve enhanced PDT effects under acidic conditions. Besides, FRNPs-pH systems, such as sensor films consisting of three dyes in the sensor layers based on RGB technique [84], can perform both ratiometric FL sensing of pH in vivo and imaging-guided monitoring of acute/chronic wound healing on the plain skin surface of volar forearm, which indicates significant therapeutic applications.

Before biological and medical applications in vitro and in vivo, the biocompatibility of various advanced functionalized materials prepared experimentally is required to evaluate carefully. High biocompatibility and low bio-toxicity of newly explored functionalized materials are fundamental prerequisites for biomedicine applications. Hence, biocompatibility and bio-toxicity of FRNPs-pH prepared in previous experiments are extensively investigated before in-vitro/in-vivo pH-sensing, imaging and therapeutic applications. Usually, prior to intracellular sensing and imaging detection of pH, the in-vitro cytotoxicity of different FRNPs-pH systems is evaluated by using normal and tumor cell lines based on MTT assays. The cell lines are continuously incubated with FRNPs-pH for a relatively long time. If no obvious decrease in cell viability is observable for the cells treated with FRNPs-pH at a relatively high coexisting concentration, the results indicate FRNPs-pH with low bio-toxicity in vitro [21,25,32,34,35,43,46,51,54,65,74,77,79,81,182]. Specifically, before cell imaging, the toxicity of dual-emission CDs as ratiometric FL nanoprobes of pH were evaluated by MTT assays [25]. No significant decline in cell viability was found for HeLa cells treated with the CDs at a high concentration of 0.5 mg/mL ( Fig. 17 A). As for the CDs prepared from hydrothermal treatments of citric acid and basic fuchsin, lower in-vitro toxicity and satisfactory biocompatibility were experimentally testified. These results indicate a high suitability and safety of the CDs for FL sensing and imaging detection of pH in living cells.

Fluorescent Eu@SiNRs-based ratiometric sensors were constructed for real-time measurements of intracellular pH in live cells [46]. The cytotoxicity of Eu@SiNRs to HeLa and MCF-7 cells was evaluated by measuring cellular mitochondrial activity via MTT assay. Viability of cells treated by Eu@SiNRs with different concentrations for 24 h maintains above 100%, together with negligible changes in cell morphologies (Fig. 17B). Eu@SiNRs may act as free radical scavengers to remove reactive oxygen species (ROS), which thereby inhibits cell apoptosis to promote cell proliferation to some extent. These cytotoxicity data suggest that Eu@SiNRs are noncytotoxic or low cytotoxic, and can be safely used for cellular studies. Biomass quantum dots (BQDs) were prepared based on Osmanthus leaves with folic acid (FA) and polyoxyethylene diamine. The prepared BQDs-FA as a multifunctional nanoprobe enables the monitoring of mitochondrion pH changes in cancer cells by ratiometric FL imaging [43]. Upon BQDs-FA concentration reaching up to 13 μg/mL, the survival rates of T24 and 7702 cells without laser irradiation are still close to 90% (Fig. 17 C). Under laser irradiation, the mitochondria-targeting BQDs-FA in mitochondrion can induce ROS generation to promote cancer apoptosis based on MTT assays and flow cytometric analysis. Except for targeted PDT of cancer cells induced by BQDs-FA, the FRNPs-pH (AuNC@F-mSiO2 @R-copolymer [79]) can perform chemotherapy and photothermal therapy via flow cytometry analysis and MTT tests (Fig. 17D). Dual-emissive heteroatom-doped D-CDs were explored for ratiometric FL detection of pH in living cells [22]. Cellular proliferation of MCF-7 cells treated with D-CDs shows a content- dependent manner. However, even if a dose-dependent decline, the viability of normal HEK-293 cells treated with D-CDs at a concentration up to 1000 μg/mL still retains over 60% (Fig. 17E). In comparison to normal cells with a relatively higher pH, D-CDs are easily internalized into cancer cells with relatively acidic environments, which therefore trigger much accumulation of D-CDs in cancer cells and relatively significant cytotoxicity.

After internalization via the incubation of living cells with FRNPs-pH and intravenous injection of FRNPs-pH into live small animals, the bio-distribution performances of FRNPs-pH in vitro and in vivo are widely evaluated. Typically, FITC-RB functionalized Janus SiO2 NPs were explored as a ratiometric FL probe for intracellular pH detection [77]. This probe has good dispersibility in the whole BEAS 2B cells, rather than specific organelles ( Fig. 18 A). pH values of different regions in live normal cells can be accurately determined by ratiometric FL imaging. The results imply great potential of this probe for monitoring of pH fluctuations inside living cells. As for the RhB-PEG8- K(PpIX) as a ratiometric theranostic nanoprobe of RPP for pH FL imaging-guided PDT [182], the in-vivo tumor accumulation of RPP was investigated after intravenous injection. After 0.5 h post- administration, remarkable FL aggregation of RPP is observable in the tumor tissue of 4T1 tumor- bearing mice models (Fig. 18B). FL signal is gradually enhanced with the extending time and then reduces slowly. Significant tumor accumulation of RPP is still found after 32 h, implying excellent biocompatibility of RPP during the blood circulation. Remarkable FL aggregation observed in the bladder reveals effective metabolism of RPP in vivo. Apparent FL aggregation appears in the liver because of RPP metabolism in the primary metabolic organ of the liver. However, RPP exhibits a significant accumulation in the tumor based on the relative mean FL intensity analysis of different major organs and tissues of tumor-bearing mice.

Considering that FRNPs-pH systems show facile construction methods, adjustable compositions, structures, properties, functions and promising applications, especially in bio-sensing, bio-imaging, biomedical diagnosis and treatments at the in-vitro and in-vivo levels, this present review conducts a systematic, comprehensive, in-depth summary and analysis for this currently important research direction of FRNPs-pH, and provides relevant research conclusions, discussions and prospects in the subsequent researches.

With regard of dual-emission FL ratiometric modes, two FL signals are converted into a ratio of F1/F2. A function curve equation, frequently referring to linear or logarithmic curve, is constructed by plotting the relationship between F1/F2 and pH. In the plotted curve equation of “f(F1/F2)=a×g (pH)+b”, a larger slope of the curve indicates that a smaller variation of pH can cause a significant change of F1/F2. If a smaller change of pH or g(pH) induces an obvious change of F1/F2 or f(F1/F2), the results reveal a high sensitivity of the resulting FRNPs-pH during the process of pH detection. A smaller variation of pH unit results in the corresponding changes of F1 or/and F2. If the change of F1/F2 ratio is larger upon a slight variation of pH unit, the constructed FRNPs-pH systems based on the relationship of F1/F2 versus pH exhibit a higher sensitivity for pH detection. In contrast, one of critical factors is to require remarkable changed F1/F2 ratio values, especially including F1↑/F2↓, F1↑/F2↔, F1↔/F2↓, etc. Dual-emission FL signals are utilized to construct the ratio processing and achieve a ratiometric output mode of dual-FL signals. The principal purpose is to eliminate system detection errors under equal measurement conditions, so as to perform in-built and self-calibration processing of detection signals, and avoid potential influence caused by system self-luminescence, background signals, and manual operations in the process of sample preparation steps. In contrast, under single-wavelength excitation, as for the same sample, slight pH variation can cause obvious changes in the dual-FL signals, which can construct excellent F1/F2 dual-signal ratiometric output. However, if two FL signals are derived from the same sample, but separately measured at different excitation wavelengths, or if two FL signals from different samples are separately measured at the same excitation wavelength, the resulting dual-FL ratiometric outputs cannot effectively eliminate possible effects of pH-unrelated factors, and cannot improve the accuracy of pH detection.

With regard of the construction methods of FRNPs-pH, numerous previous studies develop two major types of FRNPs-pH systems, which frequently involve two forms of main frame materials, including fluorescent nanomaterials and inorganic/organic hybrid materials. At various conditions of pH values, reversible electron gaining-losing reactions of specific fluorescent groups in organic molecules can result in FL changes of specific organic fluorophores in organic molecule emitters. Slight variation of pH in a systemic environment can result in the corresponding reactions among internal components or surface active groups of the functionalized nanomaterials. The pH-induced reactions will further trigger the changes, in relation to electronic bands, microstructures, existing forms or FL properties of the nanomaterials. Based on specific organic molecule systems carrying fluorophores and fluorescent inorganic materials, inorganic/organic hybrid materials with various composition forms and adjustable luminescence features, can be constructed flexibly, and greatly enrich the constructed types of FRNPs-pH systems. In forthcoming researches, the development of novel organic molecules and polymers containing pH-responsive fluorophores is one of important research directions. Under guidance of theoretical calculations, a new type of molecular structures is reasonably designed. Then, rational preparation and performance optimization experiments are performed to obtain the novel pH-responsive organic fluorophores. The subsequent researches are required to involve the design and preparation of novel pH-responsive fluorescent nanomaterials, including single atom systems, low-dimensional materials doping with heteroatoms, and materials with different dimensional heterostructures. Besides, novel FRNPs-pH systems can be constructed based upon specific interactions between new functional nanomaterials and organic fluorophores. The exploration of pH-responsive fluorescent inorganic/organic hybrid materials can be performed by adopting advanced assembly technologies, such as electro-spinning, nanopore arrays, patterned assembly and artificial intelligence, so as to develop new FRNPs-pH systems.

With regard of potential applications of FRNPs-pH, previous studies have extensively reported pH-biosensing, bioimaging, biomedical diagnosis or treatment applications of various FRNPs-pH systems at both in vitro and in vivo levels. These applications majorly refer to FL imaging at the levels of living cells and small animals, pH-sensing and imaging detection at the levels of whole cells, organelles, tissues and organs, as well as FL imaging-guided therapy of tumors. In following researches, to enhance the application efficiency of FRNPs-pH, it is necessary to introduce various functionalized components, such as active bio-labeling probes, drug molecules, anticancer agents, photo/electro-catalysts and multifunctional substrates, to the compositions and structure designs of FRNPs-pH systems. The FRNPs-pH systems will be endowed with multifunctional properties and higher application efficiencies. Except for pH-sensing applications, versatile FRNPs-pH systems can perform in-situ and real-time sensing and imaging detection of other target ions or molecules, perform visualization and imaging monitoring for the entire process in biomedical diagnosis and disease treatment, and achieve new and promising applications of nanoprobe systems in the photo/ electro-catalysis and energy conversion fields. Rational design of the composition and structure of FRNPs-pH, optimized preparation strategy and experimental operation help to achieve synergistic enhancement effects of multiple functions in FRNPs-pH systems, and achieve efficient application in the process of disease treatment. Of course, as for the newly developed multifunctional FRNPs- pH systems, it is necessary to conduct careful researches on both bio-toxicity and bio-distribution before biomedical applications. A low or ultralow cytotoxicity of the host material itself from the FRNPs-pH systems ensures the intensely required high biocompatibility, biosafety and feasibility for subsequent in-vitro/in-vivo sensing and imaging applications. Appropriate in-vivo experiments are conducted to investigate the distribution of FRNPs-pH in living small animals through tracing the distribution status in various tissues, organs or tumors. Time-dependent metabolism behaviors are required to monitor, so as to evaluate the metabolizability capability and in-vivo residual levels of FRNPs-pH during long-term blood circulation. A high metabolism capability and a low residual level of FRNPs-pH are expected during the in-vivo therapeutic applications. These requirements acting as principal prerequisites, should be carefully considered before the design, preparation and biomedical applications of various newly explored multifunctional FRNPs-pH systems. They also guide the high-quality and orderly development of FRNPs-pH related studies, especially referring to composition selection, structure design, functional modification, etc.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rijun Gui received his BS degree in 2005, and then completed his MS degree in Applied Chemistry from Shanghai Ocean University in 2009. Afterward, he studied at East China University of Science and Technology to pursue his PhD degree (2012) in Physical Chemistry. After that, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow at Shanghai Jiao Tong University. In 2014, he joined in the Department of Chemistry at Qingdao University, where he is currently an associate professor. His research interests focus on the design, preparation, property, multifunctional modification and application studies about luminescent and electro-active composites, organic/inorganic hybrids and multiple functional materials. Recently, he is focusing on the systematic studies on versatile material systems that enable potential applications in chemo/bio-sensing, bio-imaging and bio-medicine fields. So far, he has published over 90 scientific papers with citation of over 4500, H-index of over 40, and ESI highly cited papers of over 3.

Hui Jin received her BS degree in 2005, and completed her MS degree in Applied Chemistry from Shanghai Ocean University in 2008. Then, she studied at Tongji University to purse her PhD degree in Organic Chemistry. After completing her PhD degree in 2012, she worked as a research assistant at Shanghai Jiao Tong University. In 2015, she joined in Qingdao University and worked as a postdoctoral fellow. Currently, she is an associate professor in Qingdao University. Her research interests focus on preparation, modification and application studies of advanced functional nanomaterials with luminescence properties and electronic activities.
